AI.tldr: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) are prone to producing hallucinations due to language priors overriding visual inputs. FADE, a training-free method, attenuates FFN outputs to address this issue effectively.
AI.motivation: Address the problem of hallucinations in LVLMs caused by language priors dominating visual inputs.
AI.method: Analyze the information flow in transformer layers and propose FADE, which attenuates FFN module outputs to limit language-prior dominance.
AI.result: FADE reduces hallucinations across various benchmarks while maintaining inference efficiency.
AI.conclusion: FADE successfully mitigates hallucinations in LVLMs, enhancing reliability without compromising operational efficiency.

title: LC-ICL: Label-Guided Contrastive In-Context Learning for Robust Information Extraction
summary: There has been increasing interest in exploring the capabilities of advanced large language models (LLMs) in the field of information extraction (IE), specifically focusing on tasks related to named entity recognition (NER) and relation extraction (RE).Although researchers are exploring the use of few-shot information extraction through in-context learning with LLMs, they tend to focus only on using correct or positive examples for demonstration, neglecting the potential value of incorporating incorrect or negative examples into the learning process.In this paper, we present LC-ICL a novel few-shot technique that leverages both correct and incorrect sample constructions to create in-context learning demonstrations. This approach enhances the ability of LLMs to extract entities and relations by combining positive samples with negative samples annotated by error-cause labels. These labels expose more detailed error features in erroneous examples, enabling the model to understand why similar predictions fail and avoid repeating such errors during inference.Specifically, our proposed method taps into the inherent contextual information and valuable information in hard negative samples and the nearest positive neighbors to the test and then applies the in-context learning demonstrations based on LLMs. Our experiments on various datasets indicate that LC-ICL outperforms previous few-shot in-context learning methods, delivering substantial enhancements in performance across a broad spectrum of related tasks. These improvements are noteworthy, showcasing the versatility of our approach in diverse scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LC-ICL, a novel few-shot learning method combining positive and negative examples to enhance large language models in information extraction (NER and RE).
AI.motivation: To address the neglect of using incorrect or negative examples in few-shot information extraction with in-context learning, and to improve LLMs' ability in named entity recognition (NER) and relation extraction (RE).
AI.method: LC-ICL combines correct and incorrect (negative) samples with annotated error-cause labels into in-context learning demonstrations, leveraging hard negative samples and nearest positive neighbors for enhanced performance.
AI.result: Experiments show that LC-ICL significantly outperforms prior few-shot methods, demonstrating improved performance across various datasets and tasks.
AI.conclusion: LC-ICL proves to be an effective few-shot approach that enhances LLMs' understanding of errors and improves their robustness and versatility in information extraction tasks.

title: Multi-UAV Formation Cooperative Obstacle Avoidance and Adaptive Shape Deformation Control in Complex Environments Based on BI-APF-RRT and Affine Transformation
summary: Aiming at the problem that obstacle avoidance flexibility and formation integrity are difficult to coexist in multi-UAV formation motion in complex obstacle environments , and that the traditional artificial potential field (APF) method easily falls into local optima, a cooperative obstacle avoidance algorithm for multi-UAV formations integrating BI-APF-RRT and affine transformation is proposed. First, abandoning the traditional APF centroid path planning method , a goal-biased Bidirectional Artificial Potential Field method RRT (BI-APF-RRT) algorithm is adopted to conduct global collision-free path planning for the centroid of the leader formation. By introducing an improved artificial potential field and cubic B-spline interpolation, the smoothness and rapid convergence of the global path are ensured. Secondly, using the generated global path as the guiding trajectory for the formation's centroid , combined with an affine transformation matrix (including non-uniform scaling and rotation) , the formation can adaptively deform based on the distance to obstacles while moving along the optimal path. Finally, the followers track the leaders through a distributed control law , enabling the entire formation to safely cross complex obstacle areas without disassembling.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a cooperative obstacle avoidance algorithm for multi-UAV formations using BI-APF-RRT and affine transformation to improve flexibility and formation integrity in complex environments.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the difficulties of balancing obstacle avoidance flexibility and maintaining formation integrity for multi-UAV systems within complex obstacle-filled environments, and overcoming the limitations of traditional APF methods which can fall into local optima.
AI.method: The proposed method uses BI-APF-RRT for global path planning of the leader formation centroid, improves it with artificial potential fields and cubic B-spline interpolation for smoothness and fast convergence, and applies affine transformations to adapt formation shape while navigating.
AI.result: The method allows UAV formations to perform flexible obstacle avoidance while maintaining formation structure, enabling safe passage through complex environments.
AI.conclusion: By integrating BI-APF-RRT and affine transformation, the approach achieves effective global path planning and adaptive formation control, overcoming the weaknesses of traditional APF methods and ensuring formation safety and integrity.

title: When Can Conformal Risk Control Certify LLM Outputs? Bounds, Impossibility, and Adaptation for Structured Generation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) deployed for structured generation (NER, JSON extraction, QA, and classification) lack formal reliability guarantees, and standard heuristic abstention policies miss user-specified risk targets by 7.5--12.5%. We characterize when conformal risk control (CRC) can certify structured LLM outputs and when it provably cannot. First, we prove an impossibility result: when the base risk (μ> α), any distribution-free method must abstain on at least ((μ-α)/(1-α)) examples, yielding a closed-form feasibility test: one can check whether CRC will work before running it. Second, we analyze a certification hierarchy across Hoeffding, empirical Bernstein, and a betting-based e-CRC bound, with strict gains in low-variance/large-sample regimes: the Hoeffding-to-Bernstein step delivers the largest gain (+37% certified configurations), while e-CRC adds value when calibration data is scarce (10% certification at 20% data versus 0% for Hoeffding). Third, we validate adaptive conformal inference (ACI) under cross-dataset shift, reducing risk-target violations from 71% to 21%, with residual failures concentrated exactly where the impossibility bound predicts. Across six open-weight models (3B--72B parameters), eight datasets, four tasks, and six nonconformity scores, hard NER/QA/CLS configurations are uncertifiable at (α= 0.10); relaxing to (α= 0.30--0.40) unlocks practical certification (47% NER, 40% QA, 60% CLS). The framework gives a three-step deployment recipe: check feasibility, select the bound and score, then mitigate shift.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the unreliability of large language models (LLMs) in structured generation tasks by introducing Conformal Risk Control (CRC) to certify outputs, highlighting conditions where such certification is possible and providing a deployment framework.
AI.motivation: LLMs excel in tasks like NER, JSON extraction, QA, and classification but lack guaranteed reliability. Existing heuristic abstention policies fail to meet user-defined risk thresholds, necessitating methods for certifiable reliability.
AI.method: The study provides a theoretical feasibility test for CRC, evaluates certification bounds (Hoeffding, Bernstein, and e-CRC), and tests adaptive conformal inference (ACI) under cross-dataset shifts to improve risk control in LLM outputs.
AI.result: The Hoeffding-to-Bernstein bound achieves the largest certification gains (+37% configurations), while e-CRC proves useful with limited calibration data. ACI reduced cross-dataset shift violations significantly (71% to 21%). Certain configurations become uncertifiable at a 10% risk threshold but practical at 30–40%.
AI.conclusion: The three-step framework offers a pathway for deploying LLMs for structured tasks: test feasibility, select appropriate bounds and scores, and mitigate dataset shifts. Results demonstrate the potential for practical certification under adjusted risk thresholds.

title: ITSPACE: Monotone Gaussian Optimal Transport Updates
summary: Covariance matrices serve as compact descriptors of feature distributions in many machine-learning pipelines, including domain adaptation and Gaussian embeddings. Under a centered Gaussian approximation, the unregularized Wasserstein-2 optimal-transport (OT) discrepancy admits a closed form on covariances given by the Bures-Wasserstein (BW) objective on the symmetric positive definite (SPD) cone. We propose ITSPACE (Iterative Transport for Stable Proximal Alignment of Covariance Embeddings), a proximal majorization-minimization method that directly optimizes this exact BW objective through closed-form updates in a square-root factorization. In exact arithmetic, each iteration satisfies a sufficient-decrease inequality for the BW objective; under inexact polar computations, we provide an explicit certificate-gap bound controlling deviations from exact descent. The resulting iterations preserve PSD structure by construction and naturally support rank-restricted factors, making ITSPACE well-suited as a lightweight inner-loop primitive in settings where adaptation must be performed from unlabeled target batches under strict step and compute budgets. Across real-world covariance-alignment benchmarks, ITSPACE reaches low-BW-gap solutions substantially faster than BW-gradient descent, methods based on other covariance geometries, and entropically regularized sample-OT baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ITSPACE, a proximal majorization-minimization method for efficiently optimizing the Bures-Wasserstein objective on covariance matrices, achieving faster convergence and better performance compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: Covariance matrices are widely used in machine learning to describe feature distributions, and optimizing the Bures-Wasserstein (BW) objective can improve tasks like domain adaptation. Existing methods struggle with efficiency and performance under practical constraints.
AI.method: The authors propose ITSPACE, a method that uses proximal majorization-minimization with closed-form updates on square-root factorizations of covariance matrices. It ensures strict PSD preservation during iterations and supports rank-restricted factors, making it highly efficient.
AI.result: ITSPACE outperforms BW-gradient descent and other baseline approaches in terms of computational speed and solution quality across real-world benchmarks for covariance alignment.
AI.conclusion: ITSPACE is a practical and computationally efficient solution for optimizing the BW objective, particularly suited for real-world scenarios with strict computational and step constraints.

title: X-Mind: Efficient Visual Chain-of-Thought via Predictive World Model for End-to-End Driving
summary: Predicting future states is essential for autonomous agents, yet current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models fundamentally lack this capability, relying instead on reactive perception-action mapping. While integrating Predictive World Models (PWMs) addresses this gap, existing approaches either incur prohibitive cascaded latency or act as shallow terminal tasks that fail to deeply embed forward-looking reasoning. To endow VLA models with this reasoning capability, we propose X-Mind. Rather than treating PWMs as an external auxiliary module, this framework internalizes them as the Visual Chain-of-Thought (Visual CoT). By enforcing a world rollout prior to action, the model is constrained to imagine future evolution first, yielding a driving policy that is robustly grounded in environmental dynamics and aware of the future consequences its actions will unfold. The challenge here is efficiency, and we tackle it on two fronts. First, we introduce a compact representation of visual thinking: an abstract sketch that fuses a Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) layout with abstract driving priors (e.g., navigation intents and traffic rules). Rather than rolling out dense future frames, the model reasons over this sketch as a mental canvas; aided by a Deep Compression Autoencoder (DC-AE), a 12-frame future rollout is reduced to merely 96 tokens, alleviating the long-context computational bottleneck. Second, to accelerate generation further, we propose a recurrent block diffusion scheme that unrolls the denoising steps across the layers of the large drive model, folding iterative refinement into the backbone's one forward pass. Trained and validated on large-scale real-world data, X-Mind achieves competitive end-to-end driving performance, which makes it a highly practical, low-latency solution that successfully deploys large-scale cognitive reasoning directly onto resource-constrained vehicle platforms.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces X-Mind, a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model that integrates predictive reasoning for autonomous driving by internalizing Predictive World Models as a Visual Chain-of-Thought and using efficient compact representations to enhance performance.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models for autonomous agents lack predictive reasoning and rely on reactive response systems, prompting a need for more forward-looking and robust driving policies.
AI.method: The X-Mind framework integrates Predictive World Models as Visual Chain-of-Thought, using a compact Bird’s-Eye-View (BEV) sketch representation and a Deep Compression Autoencoder to efficiently simulate future outcomes. A recurrent block diffusion scheme further accelerates action inference.
AI.result: X-Mind achieves competitive performance in end-to-end autonomous driving, demonstrating practical deployment capabilities on resource-constrained vehicle platforms.
AI.conclusion: The proposed X-Mind model effectively internalizes predictive reasoning, offering a low-latency and efficient solution for autonomous driving, with strong integration of forward-looking environmental dynamics and future consequence awareness.

title: How Much Due Diligence Before You Bid? Learning in Intractable Takeover Auctions
comment: 21 pages, 13 figures, 2 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/zainnab-sparq/imperfect-information-deal-games
summary: When two companies bid to buy the same target, no one knows exactly what the target is worth. Each bidder pays for due diligence: costly, imperfect homework that sharpens its own private estimate before it bids. How much of that homework is worth buying? We build a simple computer model of the bidding contest and let it teach itself to bid well by playing against itself, the way a game engine learns chess. The economic question, how much diligence pays for itself, and the computational question, when the contest becomes too complex to solve exactly, are both controlled by a single thing: how many pieces of private information a bidder carries. Our main finding is that the right amount of diligence is modest and finite. It falls as diligence gets more expensive, and it falls further when both sides are doing their homework, because competition erodes the value of knowing more. We also test a recent claim from AI research: that simple, general self-play methods can rival the specialized, expensive algorithms usually built for games like these. Running on an ordinary laptop with no costly frontier AI, we find the simple methods are the best of the self-learning approaches, though purpose-built exact methods still win whenever the game is small enough to solve outright. The simple methods earn their keep only once the game grows too large to solve exactly, which is the regime real deals live in, and there we show they still find strong bidding strategies. The contribution is threefold: a cheap, reproducible way to study deal-making under uncertainty; a concrete, model-based answer to how much due diligence is worth buying; and evidence about when lightweight, general-purpose AI is good enough to replace specialized methods. We release all the games, code, and experiments.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how much due diligence is worth investing in during bidding contests between companies using self-learning AI to model the process.
AI.motivation: The authors want to understand the value of due diligence in corporate bidding scenarios and assess when lightweight AI is sufficient compared to specialized algorithms.
AI.method: They created a computer model for bidding contests, allowing it to teach itself via self-play to determine optimal diligence and bidding strategies.
AI.result: The model found that modest diligence is optimal and its value decreases when due diligence is expensive or when competitors also conduct diligence. Lightweight self-learning AI performed well in complex scenarios but was outperformed by specialized methods in simpler games.
AI.conclusion: The study offers a cheap, reproducible way to analyze uncertain deal-making, provides guidance on due diligence investment, and evaluates the effectiveness of general-purpose AI in these scenarios.

title: EntroRouter: Learning Efficient Model Routing via Entropy Regulation
summary: Model routing balances solution accuracy and computational cost by selecting among models of varying capabilities. While recent multi-round frameworks interleave reasoning and planning, we identify a structural failure mode termed Trust Region Collapse. We demonstrate that the deep coupling of reasoning and routing, exacerbated by the dominance of strong pre-training priors under sparse supervision, leads to degenerate local optima where capable experts are systematically suppressed. To decouple these processes, we propose $\textbf{EntroRouter}$, a single-round routing framework that treats entropy regulation as a core objective. We first initialize the policy via Soft Supervision, fitting a distribution of suitable models to establish a high-entropy prior for exploration. Subsequently, we stabilize Reinforcement Learning using a Soft Anchor, which utilizes offline capability estimates to orchestrate controlled entropy contraction within a safe trust region. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EntroRouter retains 98.3% of the strongest expert's accuracy while reducing computational costs by 48.25%.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses inefficiencies in multi-round model routing by introducing EntroRouter, a single-round framework balancing performance and computational cost.
AI.motivation: The study aims to tackle Trust Region Collapse, a structural failure in multi-round model routing, where strong models are underutilized due to tight coupling between reasoning and routing processes.
AI.method: The proposed EntroRouter employs a single-round routing approach, initializing policies with Soft Supervision for a high-entropy exploration and using a Soft Anchor to stabilize Reinforcement Learning within a safe trust region.
AI.result: EntroRouter achieves 98.3% of the accuracy of the most capable model while reducing computational costs by nearly half (48.25%).
AI.conclusion: By decoupling reasoning and routing and focusing on entropy regulation, EntroRouter offers a robust solution to optimize both accuracy and efficiency in model routing.

title: Cross-Spectral Stereo Inertial Odometry
summary: Standard stereo VIO focuses exclusively on the benefit of metric scale via single-spectrum baselines, often overlooking the risks of spectral redundancy. This structural limitation leads to correlated failures, where both sensors simultaneously fail in degraded environments that affect their shared spectrum. Leveraging a cross-spectral system presents a complementary solution to this issue, yet the significant appearance gap between modalities renders standard matching ineffective. Existing deep learning-based matchers, while effective, introduce inference latencies that violate real-time constraints. To bridge this gap, we present an asynchronous real-time cross-spectral visual-thermal-inertial (VTI) system that temporally decouples high-latency deep matching from high-rate state estimation. Our architecture incorporates a spectral-aware weighting scheme that dynamically balances modality reliance based on photometric entropy and thermal noise, ensuring robustness against both abrupt lighting changes and thermal artifacts. Furthermore, we introduce a seamless handling mechanism for thermal Non-uniformity Correction (NUC) to maintain tracking continuity. Extensive experiments across diverse scenarios confirm that our system overcomes spectral redundancy, yielding superior accuracy in nominal daylight while ensuring robustness in visually degraded environments. We will open source our code and data: https://github.com/seungsang07/cross-spectral-stereo-inertial-odometry

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a real-time cross-spectral VTI system that addresses the limitations of standard stereo VIO through a decoupled architecture for robust state estimation across challenging environments.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the limitations of standard stereo VIO systems, which suffer correlated failures in degraded environments due to their reliance on a single spectrum.
AI.method: A cross-spectral VTI system is proposed with an asynchronous architecture, balancing high-latency deep matching and high-rate state estimation using a spectral-aware weighting scheme and handling thermal corrections.
AI.result: The system demonstrated improved accuracy in normal conditions and enhanced robustness in challenging environments through experiments.
AI.conclusion: Cross-spectral solutions effectively resolve the risk of spectral redundancy in VIO systems, providing a robust alternative for real-time and degraded condition navigation.

title: Statistically Indistinguishable, Operationally Distinct: A Formal Barrier for Tabular Foundation Models
comment: Accepted at the 2nd ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data, 2026
summary: Tabular foundation models cannot reason about data produced by running systems without access to the rules that govern them. We make this statement falsifiable. The \emph{Operational Turing Test} (OTT) constructs pairs of legal and rule-violating database states whose $1$- and $2$-way column-value marginals match to a total variation of $<0.02$; Le~Cam's lemma then bounds any values-only classifier at $\geq0.49$ Bayes error. Three values-only baselines (XGBoost, TabICL, TabPFN) hit the bound exactly (accuracy $0.50$, pre-registered two one-sided tests (TOST) $p<0.002$), raw row-level access does not help, exposing relational value consistency closes most of the gap, and only a classifier fed by seven executable rule-derived audits reaches $1.00$ classification accuracy. In three matched $100$-state frontier large-language-model (LLM) runs, models given the schema, trigger source, rule tables, and state files classify at most $2/50$ legal states as LEGAL; GPT-5.5 accepts $0/50$ legal states even with higher reasoning effort and a Structured Query Language (SQL) executor. The access-ladder pattern also appears on a second schema with structurally distinct rule families (banking ledger: cross-row balance, cumulative aggregate). The barrier is identifiability, not capacity: scale, data, and richer features cannot cross it without operational grounding.

AI.tldr: Tabular foundation models struggle with reasoning on datasets governed by operational rules unless they have access to rule-derived audits or operational grounding.
AI.motivation: Investigate the reasoning limitations of tabular foundation models in contexts where data is governed by complex, executable rules.
AI.method: Introduce the Operational Turing Test (OTT) for falsifiability, pair database states with matching value marginals, test classifiers under various access conditions, and evaluate legal state classification performance for tabular models and large language models.
AI.result: Values-only classifiers hit a near-random bound of 0.50 accuracy, and providing rule-derived audits closes the reasoning gap. Tabular and LLM models show poor performance unless operational grounding is provided.
AI.conclusion: Data reasoning requires access to rule-derived audits; models relying solely on data features or schema remain inadequate without operational grounding for rule-based contexts.

title: Convergence of Continual Learning in Homogeneous Deep Networks
summary: We characterize weakly regularized continual classification in homogeneous models as sequential projections onto task margin sets. This result generalizes prior analyses restricted to either stationary (single-task) deep models or continual linear models. We show that global convergence generally fails, even for simple models linear in data but nonlinear in parameters. Nevertheless, by leveraging results from nonconvex projection theory, we identify regularity properties of homogeneous deep networks that guarantee local linear convergence under random and cyclic task sequences. Finally, we extend our analysis to continual regression, unifying the framework for homogeneous models.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the behavior of weakly regularized continual classification in homogeneous models, generalizing prior findings and demonstrating local linear convergence under specific conditions.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of analytical characterization for weakly regularized continual classification in deep homogeneous models compared to stationary or linear models.
AI.method: The authors use nonconvex projection theory to study sequential projections onto task margin sets and assess regularity properties of homogeneous deep networks.
AI.result: They find that global convergence often fails but identify conditions under which local linear convergence occurs for random and cyclic task sequences in classification tasks.
AI.conclusion: The framework is extended to continual regression, offering a unified analytical foundation for homogeneous models under continual learning tasks.

title: Stochastic Optimal Control Sampling for Diffusion Inverse Problems
summary: Benefiting from the strong ability to capture data distributions, diffusion models have become powerful tools for solving image inverse problems. The key is to controllably steer the sampling trajectory toward the measurements while respecting the diffusion prior. In this work, we introduce Stochastic Optimal Control Sampling (SOCS), which models the denoising process as a dynamical system and injects control signals via SOC. Previous SOC-based approach addresses inverse problems by optimizing over the entire trajectory, which is computationally expensive. In contrast, we derive a closed-form control update and apply it at each sampling step, pulling the measurement-consistent clean prediction back onto the denoising flow. In SOCS, we can readily modulate the control strength to align with the diffusion model's native capabilities and thereby enhance perceptual quality. Our method is compatible with a variety of linear stochastic differential equation backbones. Extensive experiments across a broad spectrum of image inverse tasks demonstrate that SOCS achieves accurate measurement-aligned reconstructions with improved visual fidelity and stronger quantitative performance.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SOCS, a method for enhancing image inverse problem solutions using diffusion models and closed-form control updates at each step, achieving better reconstructions.
AI.motivation: To address computational inefficiencies and improve the quality of results in solving image inverse problems using diffusion models.
AI.method: SOCS models the denoising process as a dynamical system and uses closed-form stochastic optimal control updates to modulate the sampling trajectory at every step.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that SOCS delivers accurate reconstructions aligned with measurements, achieving better visual fidelity and stronger quantitative results.
AI.conclusion: SOCS improves both perceptual and quantitative performance for image inverse tasks, offering an efficient and compatible framework for utilizing diffusion models.

title: Agent-Computer Observation Interfaces Enable Dynamic Computer Use
summary:
SWE-agent established the action interface as an underexplored design axis for software-engineering agents; we make the analogous case for the observation interface in computer-use (CU) agents. Current CU agents, closed and open-source alike, tie observation to action--one screenshot every 3-5 s, no audio--leaving them blind and deaf between screenshots to video, animations, transient UI events, meetings, and spoken instructions. We introduce the Agent-Computer Observation Interface (AOI), a model-agnostic perception layer that decouples continuous, adaptive observation from discrete actions through three gated components: inter-step keyframe capture, volume-gated audio transcription, and CU-model-generated visual narration that persists as text. Each produces almost nothing on static, silent content, reducing to the standard loop without degrading it.
  On DynaCU-Bench (100 dynamic browser tasks plus a 50-task static control), CU models from 7B to frontier scale gain +17 to +48 pp over their screenshot baselines with zero retraining, turning tasks that are near-impossible from periodic screenshots into largely solved ones. The gap is starkest on audio: on a spoken-content subset AOI agents solve every task, whereas streaming voice models hear accurately but cannot act on what they hear without the scaffold. The decomposition is as informative as the headline gain: keyframe selection turns out not to matter--the value comes from narrating captured frames into persistent text--and the interface is not a fixed bundle, since on a newer model (Gemini 3 Flash) the keyframe stream actively regresses through image-token dilution, so its components must be selected per model rather than shipped as one configuration.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the Agent-Computer Observation Interface (AOI), which improves computer-use agents' perception via continuous, adaptive observations like audio transcription and visual narration, enhancing their performance on dynamic browser tasks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address a limitation in current computer-use agents, which are restricted in their observation capabilities due to tight coupling with discrete action steps, rendering them ineffective in dynamic and audio-rich scenarios.
AI.method: The authors propose the AOI, a decoupled observation interface with components for capturing inter-step keyframes, transcribing audio, and converting frames into persistent text. These components adaptively observe the environment, enabling richer interaction and understanding.
AI.result: Using the DynaCU-Bench benchmark, AOI-enabled models outperform baseline models by 17–48 percentage points on dynamic tasks, solving previously unsolvable tasks, particularly audio-based ones. Key findings reveal that persistent text narration is pivotal, while interface configurations vary across models.
AI.conclusion: The AOI demonstrates the value of decoupling observation from action in CU agents, showing significant performance gains without requiring model retraining. Future implementations should customize the interface components for each specific model.

title: mamabench and mamaretrieval: Benchmarks for Evaluating Medical Retrieval-Augmented Generation in Maternal, Neonatal, and Reproductive Health
comment: 13 pages, 3 tables. Datasets and construction code linked in the paper
summary: Medical question-answering benchmarks rarely cover the maternal, neonatal, child, and reproductive-health questions a nurse-midwife asks, and, to our knowledge, no public chunk-level relevance benchmark exists for maternal-health guideline retrieval. We release two benchmarks that fill these gaps. mamabench is a scope-filtered QA set of 25,949 items assembled from seven existing expert-authored sources across multiple-choice, short-answer, and rubric-graded tracks; to help users calibrate the LLM judge that scores the rubric track, we re-scope HealthBench's physician-labelled meta-evaluation to the domain. mamaretrieval pairs 3,185 clinical queries with graded (0-6) relevance labels over a 63,650-chunk maternal-health guideline corpus, using a decomposed rubric that distinguishes a chunk that answers a query from one merely on its topic. Three decisions shape both: assemble and filter expert sources rather than author questions, grade relevance rather than binarise it, and measure and disclose the limits of the labels -- scope-classifier agreement, a frontier-judge check, and a pooling-completeness audit -- rather than treat them as an oracle. A companion paper uses the benchmarks to evaluate a deployed on-device assistant; both are released openly for research.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces two new benchmarks, mamabench and mamaretrieval, aimed at improving maternal-health question answering and guideline retrieval by leveraging expert-authored sources and graded relevance labels.
AI.motivation: The researchers aim to address the lack of public benchmarks for maternal, neonatal, child, and reproductive-health questions as well as guideline retrieval in maternal health, fields that are rarely covered in existing medical QA datasets.
AI.method: The paper introduces mamabench (a QA set sourced from expert-authored content) and mamaretrieval (clinical queries paired with graded relevance labels for guideline retrieval). Three key design decisions were made: using expert sources instead of creating questions, grading relevance numerically rather than binarizing it, and measuring label limits through specific audits.
AI.result: Two benchmarks were developed: mamabench with 25,949 QA items from seven expert sources and mamaretrieval with 3,185 clinical queries linked to graded chunk relevance from a maternal-health guideline corpus. They also provide tools for calibration and evaluation of label limits.
AI.conclusion: mamabench and mamaretrieval enhance maternal-health QA and guideline retrieval research. The benchmarks and their companion paper evaluating a deployed assistant are made openly available, providing resources to advance this domain.

title: Trajectory Optimization for Collision-Aware Redundant Robotic Multi-Axis Additive Manufacturing by Constrained Gradient Projection
summary: Redundant robotic multi-axis additive manufacturing (MAAM) enables support-free and conformal fabrication, but trajectory optimization for long-horizon paths remains challenging under strict deposition-position constraints and time-varying collision constraints. This work proposes a computational framework for collision-aware trajectory optimization in redundant robotic MAAM. We first formulate nozzle-workpiece relative kinematics using a relative Jacobian, and develop a differentiable SDF-based collision model that captures fabrication-induced geometry evolution and provides optimization gradients. The deposition position is then enforced as a hard waypoint-wise equality constraint through iterative projection onto the self-motion manifold, with the loss gradient restricted to the corresponding tangent space. Experiments on an 8-DOF robotic MAAM platform with diverse long-horizon support-free and conformal toolpaths show that our method maintains a mean nozzle-position error below 10μm, reduces maximum joint jerk by up to $77.6\%$, and eliminates all sampled collision and orientation violations. Compared with the SQP-based baseline, it achieves up to a 10.2x speedup and improved convergence. Physical fabrication experiments further verify that the resulting smooth, collision-free trajectories enable successful printing of complex geometries with fewer visible deposition artifacts.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method for optimizing trajectories in robotic multi-axis additive manufacturing to avoid collisions and meet strict deposition constraints.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address challenges in optimizing trajectories for robotic multi-axis additive manufacturing, which involve long paths, strict deposition constraints, and dynamic collision issues.
AI.method: The authors propose using relative Jacobian-based nozzle-workpiece kinematics and a differentiable SDF-based collision model. Deposition constraints are enforced via iterative projection onto the self-motion manifold, with gradients restricted to tangent space.
AI.result: The proposed framework achieves nozzle-position errors below 10μm, reduces joint jerk by up to 77.6%, eliminates collisions, and achieves up to a 10.2x speedup in convergence compared to a baseline method.
AI.conclusion: This method demonstrates effective trajectory optimization for 8-DOF robotic systems, enabling precise and collision-free additive manufacturing of complex geometries.

title: Priced Motion Through Optimal Faces: A Normal-Fan Geometry for Non-Stationary Adversarial MDPs
summary: In a changing decision problem, standard dynamic-regret analyses have often equated the cost of non-stationarity to how far loss moves. However, it is simultaneously possible for a loss sequence to travel far and retain the same optimal policy, or for a small movement in loss to force the optimal policy to change completely. Thus, the size of the movement through loss variation, transition variation, or comparator path length describe the adversary's motion, but not the cost of that motion to the control problem. For a more faithful analytic interpretation, this paper develops a normal-fan geometry for finite-horizon adversarial MDPs with fixed transitions. Occupancy measures form a polytope, and each loss vector exposes an optimal face of that polytope. Non-stationarity in rewards is therefore a path through the normal fan, where motion inside one cone leaves the optimal face unchanged, while crossing a wall may carry regret. We pose the notion of a face-crossing price, which is the minimum regret incurred by remaining on the previous optimal face under the new loss. For any learner that tracks the previous face, dynamic regret decomposes exactly into intrinsic priced face motion plus within-face selection error. The resulting theory separates consequential from harmless non-stationarity, where loss variation can be arbitrarily large at zero price, and identical one-coordinate variation can hide horizon-scale differences in regret.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a geometry-based approach to analyze non-stationarity in adversarial Markov Decision Processes, focusing on the 'face-crossing price' that quantifies effective regret beyond merely tracking changes.
AI.motivation: Understanding the true cost of non-stationarity in dynamic decision problems, where loss variation and policy changes are not equally impactful.
AI.method: Introduces a 'normal-fan geometry' framework for analyzing finite-horizon adversarial MDPs, where occupancy measures form a polytope, and loss vectors expose its optimal face.
AI.result: Decomposed dynamic-regret into intrinsic 'face-crossing price' (regret incurred due to meaningful motion in loss) plus selection error within each face.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a faithful interpretation of consequential vs. harmless non-stationarity, enabling precise modeling and separation of impactful loss variations from inconsequential ones.

title: The Fundamental Limits of Valid Transport Map Estimation
summary: Many modern generative modeling methods, including diffusion models, normalizing flows, and flow matching, estimate transport maps or plans between distributions without explicitly targeting an optimal transport (OT) map. In applications like generative modeling, the transport cost itself is irrelevant, and this makes it natural to target maps which are more tractable from either a statistical or computational standpoint. In this short note, we formalize the task of estimating any valid transport map in a rigorous minimax framework. One consequence of this framing is that it yields sample complexity lower bounds for any method whose learned object is evaluated as a transport map or plan, including flow matching and diffusion-based generative models, in settings where direct analysis would be challenging due to the analytic complexity of the methods and their target maps. We observe that, under standard, though strong, stability assumptions from the OT literature, estimating any valid transport map is statistically as hard as estimating the OT map. We complement these results with some examples showing that when these stability assumptions fail, alternative transport maps can be learned substantially more accurately than the OT map. Our minimax framing provides a rigorous foundation for understanding the statistical limits of modern transport-based generative methods and clarifies when targeting sub-optimal maps can provide real statistical advantages.

AI.tldr: The paper formalizes estimating valid transport maps in a minimax framework and reveals sample complexity bounds in generative modeling methods.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous statistical understanding of generative modeling methods involving transport maps, and to explore the advantages of targeting non-optimal transport maps.
AI.method: The paper uses a minimax framework to study the statistical limits of estimating transport maps and examines cases with and without strong stability assumptions from OT literature.
AI.result: Under strong OT stability assumptions, estimating any valid map is as hard as estimating the OT map, but without such assumptions, sub-optimal maps can be more accurately learnable.
AI.conclusion: Targeting non-optimal transport maps can offer statistical advantages, and the proposed minimax framework helps delineate these trade-offs in generative modeling methods.

title: BREIT: A Framework for Brain Stroke Reconstruction using Multi-Frequency 3D EIT
summary: Multi-Frequency Electrical Impedance Tomography (MF-EIT) is a non-invasive, low-cost modality that reconstructs electrical property distributions from boundary voltages. For stroke imaging, progress in 3D deep-learning reconstruction is limited by the lack of large-scale datasets with paired ground-truth (GT) volumes and by non-standardized pipelines for data generation, simulation, and evaluation. We introduce BREIT, a modular framework for 3D MF-EIT stroke reconstruction providing: (i) a neuroimaging-to-EIT pipeline that converts CT/MRI into frequency-dependent GT admittivity volumes; (ii) a self-contained Python 3D Complete Electrode Model (CEM) forward solver for simulating MF-EIT voltages; and (iii) a 3D D-bar implementation supporting non-uniform electrode layouts. Building on BREIT, we propose dFNO-bar, which integrates Fourier Neural Operators into D-bar by learning a mapping from scattering data $t(ξ)$ to conductivity $σ(x){=}\Re\{γ\}$. We evaluate dFNO-bar against D-bar, Deep D-bar, and Gauss--Newton reconstructions on UCLH-matched synthetic data, and observe higher brain SSIM with comparable CC across noise settings. Code and data are publicly available at: https://github.com/djahiddj13/BREIT

AI.tldr: The paper presents BREIT, a modular framework for generating and reconstructing 3D Multi-Frequency Electrical Impedance Tomography (MF-EIT) stroke data, and introduces dFNO-bar, a new neural operator-based reconstruction method. BREIT enables standardized data generation and reliable evaluation for stroke imaging.
AI.motivation: The motivation of this paper is to address challenges in stroke-imaging research caused by the lack of large-scale paired datasets and standardized pipelines for data generation, simulation, and evaluation in the field of MF-EIT.
AI.method: The authors developed BREIT, a pipeline to convert neuroimaging data into frequency-dependent admittivity volumes, simulate MF-EIT voltages using a forward solver, and implement 3D reconstruction methods supporting non-uniform electrode layouts. They also propose dFNO-bar, which leverages Fourier Neural Operators to improve image reconstruction.
AI.result: The dFNO-bar method outperforms existing approaches (D-bar, Deep D-bar, and Gauss--Newton reconstructions) in achieving higher Structural Similarity Index (SSIM) in brain imaging while maintaining comparable Correlation Coefficients (CC) under varying noise conditions.
AI.conclusion: BREIT provides a standardized, modular, and open-access solution for 3D MF-EIT data generation and reconstruction, enabling enhanced stroke imaging capabilities and supporting further research progress in this domain.

title: Faults in Our Formal Benchmarking: Dataset Defects and Evaluation Failures in Lean Theorem Proving
summary: Benchmarks for LLM-assisted theorem proving in Lean are often treated as intrinsically reliable because every solved instance comes with a machine-checked proof. However, the kernel only checks that a proof establishes a \emph{formal} statement; it does not verify that the statement faithfully encodes the intended informal problem, nor that evaluation harnesses are robust to trivial or adversarial solutions. We audit five widely used Lean theorem-proving benchmarks and their forks, using corpus-scale static checkers to surface 4,833 findings, including 398 mechanically certified issues such as counterexamples, vacuous theorems, and unsound axioms. We also document semantic defects such as missing hypotheses, problem simplification, incomplete or incorrect translations, and Lean-specific specification hazards. Beyond dataset construction, we survey evaluation-time failure modes and show, on corrected subsets, that defects can both inflate and deflate reported prover scores. We propose a fault taxonomy, a suite of automated checkers and recall-oriented semantic audit prompts, and release standards to guide the creation of formal math datasets and to make evaluation more reproducible and trustworthy. Our checkers, audit prompts, and corrected dataset snapshots are available at https://github.com/Shashi456/atp-checkers.

AI.tldr: The paper audits Lean theorem-proving benchmarks, revealing significant flaws in dataset quality and evaluation methods, offering tools for more reliable dataset construction and evaluation.
AI.motivation: Lean theorem-proving benchmarks are widely trusted for providing machine-checked proofs, but they may contain semantic and structural flaws that undermine reliability.
AI.method: The authors use corpus-scale static checkers to analyze the benchmarks for quantitative issues, semantic defects, and programmatic hazards, followed by evaluations on corrected subsets.
AI.result: The audit uncovered 4,833 issues, including 398 mechanically certified problems such as vacuous theorems and counterexamples, and highlighted evaluation biases that impact prover scores.
AI.conclusion: Enhanced benchmarks incorporating automated checkers, fault taxonomy, and standards can improve dataset reliability and evaluation robustness.

title: To Reason or to Fabricate: Reasoning Without Shortcuts via Hint-Anchored Pairwise Aggregation
summary: While reinforcement learning (RL) significantly enhances LLM reasoning, its efficacy is severely undermined by Pre-RL data overlap, where RL datasets overlap with pretraining or SFT corpora, causing models to exploit shortcuts by memorizing correct answers and fabricating post-hoc reasoning. To address this, we introduce HIPPO, a novel RL framework that integrates hint-injected aggregation with a tailored pairwise reward model. By utilizing hint injection to deliberately trigger overlap-induced behaviors, the resulting traces naturally serve as explicit anchors for pairwise comparison. This provides highly discriminable preference signals, enabling a lightweight judge model to reliably distinguish genuine reasoning deduction from shortcut-driven rationalization, while the pairwise formulation ensures stable and robust optimization compared to standard PRMs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HIPPO yields substantial improvements over standard baselines and generalizes effectively to out-of-distribution general tasks, showing it extracts authentic, transferable reasoning skills rather than superficial shortcut patterns.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the HIPPO framework, which addresses pretraining data overlap issues in reinforcement learning with LLMs. HIPPO incorporates hint-injection and a pairwise reward model to ensure genuine reasoning deduction rather than shortcuts.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve RL effectiveness for LLM reasoning by tackling pretraining data overlaps, where models exploit memorization and fabricate reasoning.
AI.method: The authors propose HIPPO, which uses hint-injected aggregation to trigger overlap behaviors and a pairwise reward model to judge the reasoning quality, ensuring stable optimization.
AI.result: HIPPO demonstrates clear improvements on standard baselines and effectively generalizes to out-of-distribution tasks, extracting genuine reasoning rather than shortcuts.
AI.conclusion: HIPPO is a robust technique for enhancing RL-based reasoning in LLMs, addressing significant issues related to data overlap and promoting transferable reasoning skills.

title: Analytic Concept-Centric Memory for Agentic Embodied Manipulation
summary: Long-horizon embodied manipulation requires agents to remember persistent objects, track changing scene states, and reuse prior interaction knowledge. However, existing agent memories are often stored as unstructured histories or embedding-based records, making it difficult to retrieve manipulation-relevant object parts, physical states, action effects, and executable skills. We propose an analytic concept-centric memory framework for agentic embodied manipulation. Our memory organizes experience around structured analytic concepts, where objects are represented by semantic parts, parametric templates, grounded poses, affordances, and manipulation states. It further connects object and scene memories with transition memory for action-induced state changes and skill memory for template-grounded and policy-grounded execution. At runtime, the agent performs structured coarse-to-fine retrieval to identify relevant objects, states, transitions, and skills, supporting state-consistent reasoning and skill reuse. Experiments on memory-dependent manipulation, articulated-object generalization, real-world memory evaluation, and ablations show that our approach improves task completion, retrieval accuracy, object re-identification, and cross-object skill generalization over unstructured and embedding-based memory baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a structured memory framework for robotic manipulation, designed to improve object tracking, scene understanding, and skill reuse in challenging, long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing memory systems for manipulation tasks often lack structure, making it difficult to identify and reuse relevant objects, states, and skills for effective execution.
AI.method: The authors propose a memory framework organized around analytic concepts such as semantic object parts, physical states, and affordances. The framework integrates scene, transition, and skill memories for action-induced changes and execution.
AI.result: The framework improves task completion rates, retrieval accuracy, object re-identification, and generalization across different objects, as demonstrated in experiments on a variety of tasks.
AI.conclusion: Structured, analytic concept-centric memory enhances the efficiency and effectiveness of embodied manipulation tasks, outperforming unstructured and embedding-based approaches.

title: DiLaServe: High SLO Attainment Serving for Diffusion Language Models
summary: Diffusion language models (DLMs) have recently emerged as a promising alternative to conventional autoregressive language models. By generating multiple tokens in parallel during each denoising step, they offer higher inference throughput while maintaining competitive quality. However, realizing these throughput gains while meeting latency SLOs in a serving system requires addressing challenges introduced by DLMs' unique characteristics. These include navigating the speed-quality tradeoff created by confidence-based denoising, choosing appropriate parallelization levels across model instances under fluctuating load, and coordinating approximate KV caching mechanisms that introduce non-uniform per-step costs. To address these challenges, we present DiLaServe, a cluster-level serving system for DLMs. DiLaServe enables deadline-aware scheduling and adaptive load control through confidence-threshold adjustment, and dynamically reconfigures the cluster by solving a quality-aware optimization problem, while explicitly modeling the step-level heterogeneity introduced by approximate KV caching. Across multiple benchmarks and real-world traces, DiLaServe improves SLO attainment by up to 56.6 percentage points and reduces end-to-end request latency by up to 46\% while incurring less than 1\% accuracy drop.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DiLaServe, a serving system for diffusion language models (DLMs), addressing challenges like speed-quality tradeoffs, parallelism, and caching issues while improving performance and latency in serving systems.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to enable high-throughput and low-latency inference for DLMs, addressing challenges like speed-quality tradeoffs, parallelization issues, and efficient caching mechanisms in serving systems.
AI.method: The authors propose DiLaServe, a cluster-level serving system that uses confidence-threshold adjustment, deadline-aware scheduling, dynamic load control, and quality-aware optimization while explicitly modeling step-level heterogeneity from approximate KV caching.
AI.result: DiLaServe achieved significant performance improvements, including up to 56.6 percentage points better SLO attainment, up to 46% reduced request latency, and less than 1% accuracy drop across various benchmarks and real-world traces.
AI.conclusion: The system successfully addresses unique DLM-related serving challenges, demonstrating significant enhancements in throughput, latency, and overall performance in serving scenarios.

title: Pessimism's Paradox: Conservative Offline Training Amplifies Reward Hacking During Online Adaptation in Reasoning Models
comment: Accepted in ICML 2026 workshop on Decision-Making from Offline Datasets to Online Adaptation: Black-Box Optimization to Reinforcement Learning
summary: Conservative offline training is widely advocated as a safe foundation for subsequent online adaptation: if a policy stays close to well-supported behaviour, the argument goes, it is less likely to exploit imperfections in a learned reward model. We challenge this intuition empirically and mechanistically. We train a Qwen3-14B policy under Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO) with three levels of conservatism ($β\in \{β_{\mathrm{lo}}, β_{\mathrm{mid}}, β_{\mathrm{hi}}\}$ derived from empirical log-ratio percentiles), then adapt each checkpoint online against a learned reward ensemble (3\,$\times$\,Qwen3-1.7B) while measuring true performance on GSM8K exact-answer accuracy. We find that \emph{higher offline conservatism monotonically increases reward-hacking damage}, measured by the Goodhart gap and its area under the curve (AUGC), with Spearman $ρ= 1.0$ across all three conditions. Mechanistic analysis reveals a three-link causal chain: (i) high-$β$ DPO compresses policy entropy, (ii) Low-entropy policies generate responses with reduced diversity, concentrating in a narrow region of the reward model's training distribution (lower pairwise cosine distance), and (iii) despite this proximity, ensemble disagreement (epistemic uncertainty) increases with $β$ and is exploited faster during online optimisation. We further fit a power-law curve to the $(β, \augc)$ data and identify a practical optimal conservatism level $β^{\star}$ that balances alignment fidelity against hacking vulnerability. Our results suggest that the field needs \emph{calibrated}, not \emph{maximal}, conservatism.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the relationship between offline training conservatism and online reward hacking, finding that higher conservatism leads to worse outcomes due to reduced response diversity and increased reward model exploitation.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to challenge the common assumption that conservative offline training is inherently safer for online adaptation, exploring its actual effects on reward model exploitation and policy performance.
AI.method: The study trains a Qwen3-14B policy under three conservatism levels (β_lo, β_mid, β_hi) using Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO). Each is adapted online against a reward ensemble; performance is evaluated on GSM8K accuracy and Goodhart gaps.
AI.result: Higher conservatism monotonically increases the damage from reward hacking, with a perfect Spearman correlation (ρ=1.0). Mechanistic analysis reveals reduced response diversity and heightened epistemic uncertainty in high-conservatism policies.
AI.conclusion: Conservatism levels in training should be carefully calibrated, as excessive conservatism increases vulnerability to reward hacking. A practically optimal conservatism level (β*) can balance alignment fidelity and hacking resilience.

title: Virtual Ring Try-On
summary: This paper presents an innovative approach that enables the users to capture their hand and try the jewel ring on their hand. The user captures the image of the hand using the React Native base GUI of the mobile application and selects the ring that the user wants to try, and the output image will have the user's hand with the ring image. This approach is implemented using a combination of MediaPipe hand point detection and YOLO-V8 custom object detection. The hand image uploaded by the user first undergoes mediapipe hand point detection. It will give the hand points and a Region of Interest mask where the ring is going to be placed. Then the ring is passed through YOLO object detection, in which ring points are detected, and background is removed. After that, using vector algebra, the angular discrepancy between the finger's reference axis and the ring's principal axis is computed. Also, ring size is rescaled according to finger thickness, preserving the aspect ratio to maintain perceptual realism. Then the ring is placed on the hand image and the output image is generated and shown on the user screen.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a mobile application where users can virtually try rings on their hands using advanced hand and object detection techniques.
AI.motivation: To create an immersive and realistic virtual try-on experience for users wanting to visualize jewelry on their hands.
AI.method: The system uses a combination of MediaPipe for hand point detection and YOLO-V8 for custom object detection. Hand points and a region for the ring are identified, and vector algebra is employed to align and rescale the ring realistically on the hand.
AI.result: The application generates an output image showcasing the user's hand with the selected ring applied, with realistic scaling and alignment.
AI.conclusion: This approach provides users with an accurate and practical tool for choosing rings, enhancing the e-commerce experience by integrating hand detection and object alignment technology for realistic visualizations.

title: Cognitive World Models for Process-Level Social Influence Evaluation
summary: Social influence dialogue changes user behavior by altering internal cognitive states. The central evaluation question is whether the user's beliefs, desires, intentions, and emotions measurably change over the course of conversation, a process-oriented criterion that neither surface-level text metrics (BLEU/ROUGE) nor single-score LLM judgments can capture. We propose the \textbf{Cog}nitive \textbf{W}orld \textbf{M}odel \textbf{(CogWM)}, an LLM-based user model that reframes multi-turn dialogue evaluation from ``what did the user say'' to ``how did the user's internal cognitive state evolves.'' CogWM jointly predicts BDI/E cognitive states and user utterances and serves as both a user simulator and an evaluation platform, using a three-tier evaluation framework that covers turn-level fidelity, trajectory-level state dynamics, and task-level composite scoring. Trained via our \textbf{S}ummarize-\textbf{a}nd-\textbf{A}llocate \textbf{(SaA)} annotation pipeline on 150,454 user-turn samples across four social influence scenarios, CogWM achieves 77.6\% emotion accuracy (2.1$\times$ over GPT-5.5). In 3600 multi-agent discrimination trials, it distinguishes six commercial agents by their cognitive influence, with Llama-4-Scout ranking first (CTS +0.233). CogWM moves social influence dialogue evaluation from terminal judgment to process tracking. We have released our code\footnote{\scriptsize Code: https://github.com/lucianma05-create/CogWM} and models\footnote{Model: https://www.modelscope.cn/models/LucianMa/CogWM-14B}.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CogWM, a cognitive world model for evaluating social influence dialogues by tracking changes in users' internal cognitive states, emphasizing process-oriented evaluation over surface-level metrics.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation methods for dialogue systems (like BLEU/ROUGE) fail to capture process-oriented effects, such as how dialogues influence users' beliefs, desires, intentions, and emotions. This work aims to establish a better evaluation method for such cognitive impacts.
AI.method: The authors propose CogWM, a framework to model and predict cognitive state evolutions during dialogues. This involves a Summarize-and-Allocate (SaA) annotation pipeline, combined with LLM-based user simulations, for multitier evaluation metrics based on turn-level fidelity, trajectory-level state changes, and task-level scoring.
AI.result: CogWM demonstrated strong performance in social influence scenarios, achieving 77.6% accuracy in emotion prediction (2.1x improvement over GPT-5.5). It effectively distinguished commercial conversational agents based on their cognitive influence in 3,600 trials, with Llama-4-Scout ranked highest.
AI.conclusion: CogWM advances dialogue evaluation by focusing on cognitive state tracking rather than solely output comparison, offering a more nuanced and effective measure of social influence dialogue performance.

title: Which Tokens Need Context? A Reference-Based Analysis of Translation Responsibility Using Fertility and Entropy
comment: This is a work in progress. An extended version with machine translation output analysis and attention correlation is in preparation
summary: When humans translate, not every word depends equally on the surrounding context. Some tokens, particularly function words like pronouns and auxiliaries, rely heavily on preceding or following sentences, while others, such as proper nouns, do not. Understanding this inherent context sensitivity is essential for evaluating whether machine translation systems use context in human-like ways. However, existing approaches to analysing context usage rely on discourse-specific test sets or model internals, making them narrow or model-dependent. We propose a post-hoc, model-agnostic framework to quantify context sensitivity at lexical and syntactic levels using two measures derived from word alignments: fertility (number of target tokens generated per source token) and entropy (stability of fertility patterns across contexts). Using reference translations for three language pairs (German $\leftrightarrow$ English, English $\rightarrow$ Hindi) under four context conditions, we show that context selectively redistributes generative responsibility from source to context tokens without altering overall fertility. Function words show the largest fertility reductions, while content words remain stable, suggesting that context resolves ambiguity rather than adding new information. Our framework provides a ground-truth characterisation of selective context usage in human translation, establishing a diagnostic baseline for evaluating machine translation models.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a model-agnostic framework to analyze context sensitivity in translation by leveraging word alignments with fertility and entropy metrics, applied to three language pairs.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a method to understand how context sensitivity affects human and machine translation, without relying on discourse-specific test sets or model-dependent approaches.
AI.method: A post-hoc, model-agnostic framework uses word alignment-derived measures (fertility and entropy) to quantify lexical and syntactic context sensitivity.
AI.result: The analysis revealed that context redistributes generative responsibility without altering fertility. Function words show reduced fertility, while content words remain unaffected, indicating that context resolves ambiguity.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers an empirical baseline to understand selective context usage in human translations and serves as a diagnostic tool for evaluating machine translation systems.

title: FalconTrack: Photorealistic Auto-Labeled Perception and Physics-Aware Vision-Based Aerial Tracking
summary: Vision-based aerial tracking is critical in GPS-denied environments. Reliable perception for tracking depends on large-scale labeled data, yet most photorealistic datasets rely on heavy manual annotation and are time-consuming to produce. We present FalconTrack, a unified perception-and-tracking framework that (i) leverages a photorealistic editable simulator for automated label generation and (ii) combines multi-head perception with physics-aware tracking for zero-shot sim-to-real transfer. FalconTrack provides an automated labeling pipeline in a Gaussian Splatting simulator that isolates target Gaussians from short object videos and composites them with randomized backgrounds to generate RGB, mask, class, and 6-DoF pose labels, producing about 10k labeled images in under 20 minutes. Using this dataset, we train a multi-head perception module with staged learning and reprojection consistency, and fuse its outputs with class-conditioned dynamics priors in an EKF for tracking. Our perception model outperforms two baselines and reaches 96-100% class accuracy in zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on three geometrically diverse objects and two environments, while maintaining consistent performance in unseen simulated and real scenes. In real hardware closed-loop visual tracking, the onboard system runs at about 25 Hz and achieves 100% success in sim-to-real F1-tenth and gate tracking in five trajectories across two environments, while a mask-centered vision baseline drops to 60% success on F1-tenth during fast out-of-view scenarios.

AI.tldr: FalconTrack is a perception-and-tracking framework using a Gaussian Splatting simulator for automated dataset generation and zero-shot sim-to-real transfer with impressive accuracy and performance in real-world environments.
AI.motivation: Reliable aerial tracking in GPS-denied environments is challenged by the need for large-scale labeled datasets, which are costly and time-consuming to produce.
AI.method: FalconTrack employs a simulator-based automated labeling pipeline to generate labeled datasets quickly, trains a multi-head perception module with advanced learning techniques, and integrates it with physics-aware tracking algorithms for real-world applicability.
AI.result: FalconTrack produces around 10k labeled images in under 20 minutes and achieves superior performance (96-100% class accuracy in zero-shot tasks) across diverse objects and environments. It delivers consistent real-world visual tracking with a high success rate (100% in certain scenarios).
AI.conclusion: FalconTrack demonstrates an effective and efficient approach to address data labeling challenges and provides robust vision-based tracking, combining simulation-based datasets with advanced perception and tracking methods.

title: PSP: Harnessing Position and Shape Priors for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Medical Image Segmentation
comment: Accepted to MICCAI 2026. This is the pre-review submitted version, not the camera-ready version. The final authenticated version will be available in the MICCAI 2026 proceedings
summary: Few-Shot Medical Image Segmentation (FSMIS) offers a powerful solution to data scarcity but struggles to generalize across different imaging modalities. This performance collapse stems primarily from the drastic texture discrepancies between domains, which mislead models trained on source-specific intensity distributions. While existing methods attempt to align frequency or local texture features, they often fail to decouple semantic structure from domain-specific appearance. To address this, we identify a critical invariance: despite distinct imaging physics, the position and geometric shape of organs remain robustly consistent across modalities. Therefore, we propose a novel framework that harnesses Position and Shape Priors (PSP) for cross-domain FSMIS. Specifically, PSP first introduces a Position Coordinate Embedding (PCE) module to inject relative spatial coordinates for rapid organ localization. Subsequently, a Shape Prototype Modulation (SPM) module constructs domain-invariant structural prototypes via explicit shape priors, effectively filtering out texture noise. Furthermore, the Hybrid-Prototype Prediction (HPP) module adaptively calibrates the support prototype to the query feature distribution, mitigating feature misalignment. Extensive experiments on two public medical imaging datasets demonstrate that PSP significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework for Few-Shot Medical Image Segmentation (FSMIS) that tackles cross-modal challenges by leveraging Position and Shape Priors (PSP) to focus on invariant geometric organ features rather than domain-specific texture.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the performance challenges in FSMIS caused by texture discrepancies between imaging modalities, which hinder the generalization of existing models.
AI.method: The proposed method incorporates three modules: Position Coordinate Embedding (PCE) for spatial organ localization, Shape Prototype Modulation (SPM) to construct domain-invariant shape prototypes, and Hybrid-Prototype Prediction (HPP) to adapt query features and mitigate misalignment.
AI.result: Experiments on two public datasets show significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods in cross-domain medical image segmentation, validating the effectiveness of the proposed PSP framework.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that leveraging geometric consistency through Position and Shape Priors leads to robust FSMIS across imaging modalities, solving the texture discrepancy challenge and advancing medical image analysis.

title: UCOB: Learning to Utilize and Evolve Agentic Skills via Credit-Aware On-Policy Bidirectional Self-Distillation
summary: Skill memories can improve agentic reinforcement learning by reusing past experience as textual guidance, but retrieved skills are not oracular: they may help in one state while misleading the same policy in another. This makes the common privileged-teacher assumption fragile, namely that a skill-conditioned prompt can be treated as a fixed teacher for the no-skill prompt. We introduce UCOB, a framework for learning to utilize and evolve agentic skills via credit-aware on-policy bidirectional self-distillation. UCOB treats skill-conditioned and no-skill prompts as two on-policy context views of the same model, compares their return-to-go within the same task and anchor state, and uses the higher-return view as the local teacher. This local credit signal internalizes useful skill-conditioned behavior, corrects misleading skill usage, and guides task/state skill memory updates, utility-aware retrieval, and reflection self-training. Experiments on agentic tasks, including ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-QA, show that UCOB outperforms skill-free RL, skill-memory baselines, and self-distillation methods across model scales, with up to 23.5 and 18.0 point gains over SOTA baselines on ALFWorld and WebShop. Ablations and analyses further validate its core mechanisms and efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces UCOB, a reinforcement learning framework that improves agentic task performance by credit-aware bidirectional self-distillation, enabling better utilization of skill memories.
AI.motivation: To address the fragility of relying on skill-conditioned prompts as fixed teachers and improve the utilization of stored skill memories in reinforcement learning tasks.
AI.method: Developed UCOB, which compares returns of skill-conditioned and unskilled prompts within tasks to identify beneficial behaviors through a local credit signal that informs skill updating and retrieval.
AI.result: UCOB significantly outperformed existing RL methods and skill-memory frameworks, achieving notable performance gains up to 23.5 points over SOTA methods in benchmarks like ALFWorld and WebShop.
AI.conclusion: UCOB enhances reinforcement learning by effectively leveraging skill memories using credit-aware mechanisms, bridging gaps in skill-conditioned guidance while reducing errors from misleading skill usage.

title: The Verbose Context Problem in Medical Records
summary: The verbose context problem occurs when structured concepts have token-inefficient textual representations. This bottleneck is acute in population health: cohort-level analysis of longitudinal patient records requires reasoning over thousands of medically-coded events, often exceeding 400K tokens in total. We present PopMedQA, a benchmark isolating this problem through computational tasks on groups of longitudinal patient records. We construct the benchmark using neopatient, a new library for language-controlled generation of artificial patient records. Through extensive ablations -- including prompting strategies, prompt compression, and agentic decomposition -- we find that domain-independent methods fail to alleviate the verbose context problem. There remains significant opportunity to exploit domain-specific structure in language model inputs for population-scale reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the verbose context problem, a token inefficiency issue in representing structured concepts, particularly in population health data. They develop PopMedQA, a benchmark, to study this issue using a generated artificial patient records dataset and test various methods for improvement.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the verbose context problem, which becomes critical when analyzing population health data due to its large and complex structured textual representations, making it difficult for language models to perform reasoning efficiently.
AI.method: The researchers developed a new benchmark (PopMedQA) built using neopatient, a library for generating artificial patient records. They conducted experiments involving prompting strategies, prompt compression, and agentic decomposition to test solutions for token inefficiency.
AI.result: Results show that current domain-independent methods fail to alleviate the verbose context problem, suggesting limitations in existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that there's a pressing need to incorporate domain-specific structural knowledge into language model inputs to improve population-scale reasoning.

title: Data-Driven Modeling and Control for Tethered Space Systems with Koopman-Informed Graphs
summary: Modeling tethered space systems is critical for advanced orbital operations. Flexible components such as tethers and space nets are integral to these systems but present significant control challenges due to their high dimensional, strongly coupled, and nonlinear dynamics. While data driven methods offer alternative modeling approaches, they frequently struggle with long term predictive stability and spatial generalization. To address this, we propose the Koopman Graph Dynamics (KGD) framework to learn the structural dynamics by integrating the global linear evolution of the Koopman operator with the local topological priors of Graph Neural Networks. Building upon this representation, we develop a KGD based Model Predictive Control strategy for tethered space systems. Subsequently, the ground experiments on flexible tether and space net demonstrate the high precision modeling capabilities of the proposed method. Crucially, the framework exhibits exceptional capacity for spatial transfer without retraining. Models trained exclusively on small configurations successfully predict and control significantly larger, unseen physical scales. Furthermore, the orbit simulations within a physics engine verify the effectiveness of the proposed approach for tethered space systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes the Koopman Graph Dynamics (KGD) framework for modeling and controlling flexible tethered space systems, integrating Koopman operator dynamics with Graph Neural Networks.
AI.motivation: Challenges in controlling tethered space systems arise from their high-dimensional, nonlinear, and coupled dynamics. Existing data-driven methods often fall short in predictive stability and spatial generalization.
AI.method: The study introduces KGD, which incorporates global Koopman operator dynamics with local Graph Neural Network priors, and applies it to Model Predictive Control of tethered space systems.
AI.result: KGD demonstrated precise modeling capabilities in ground experiments, including spatial transfer to larger, unseen configurations, and validated its effectiveness in orbit simulations.
AI.conclusion: The framework shows promise for scalable, generalizable, and effective modeling and control of tethered space systems.

title: A Novel Latent-Class Attack and its Detection by Class Subspace Orthogonalization
summary: Deep learning, which in general relies on voluminous amounts of training data, is vulnerable to data poisoning attacks, including error-generic attacks and backdoors (Trojans). In this work, we propose a new data poisoning attack we dub a latent class attack. Here, all poisoned examples are from a class that is novel (unknown) for the given classification domain and are mislabeled to one of the known classes (the target class) of the domain, so that the model learns to recognize the novel class as a sub-class of the target class. Such attacks could be used e.g. to defeat AI-based access control systems, or could cause a "foe" to be classified as a "friend".
