AI.tldr: The paper proposes a low-rank adaptation (LoRA)-tuned large language model (LLM) for early dementia detection using structured multi-view reasoning across four speech-derived signals, achieving an F1-score of 90.14%.
AI.motivation: Early detection of dementia is critical for timely intervention. Conventional approaches lack integrative reasoning across varied cognitive symptoms, and this research aims to address that limitation using a unified model.
AI.method: The proposed method uses a LoRA-tuned LLM that performs multi-view reasoning over four complementary speech-derived cues: ASR transcripts with pause markers, discourse-level topic cues, temporal fluency statistics, and phonological sequences. All cues are encoded within a unified prompt to avoid modality-specific encoders or late-stage fusion.
AI.result: The model performs with a high F1-score of 90.14% on the ADReSSo dataset, and the study's ablation confirms that each speech-derived view contributes to the model.
AI.conclusion: The research demonstrates that a single LLM, leveraging multiple complementary speech-derived signals in a structured manner, is highly effective in dementia detection.

title: Theory of Continual Learning Against Data Poisoning Attacks
summary: Continual learning (CL), where a model is trained on a sequence of data tasks, is increasingly being adopted across key fields such as large language models and image recognition, yet it remains highly vulnerable to data poisoning that triggers learning divergence or severe excess risk. Despite these threats, a principled theoretical foundation in CL for understanding attack and defense remains lacking. In this paper, we develop a theoretical framework to analyze strategic attacks and defenses in regularization-based CL, a cornerstone of recent CL theory. By framing the adversary-defender interaction as an online zero-sum game, we first establish a fundamental performance limit: no defense succeeds when an adversary poisons a linear proportion of tasks by injecting unbounded noise or pattern shifts in regularization-based CL. We then analyze two possibly defensible scenarios: infrequent attacks and bounded noise per attack. For the former regime, we propose a task-to-task verification mechanism to detect data poisoning and reduce cumulative bias for learning convergence. For the latter regime, we derive a robust defense that minimizes the model's sensitivity to poisoned features, provably accelerating the convergence rate. Extensive experiments on realistic tasks further validate our theoretical results.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a theoretical framework to understand data poisoning in continual learning (CL) and proposes defense mechanisms for specific adversarial scenarios.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the lack of theoretical understanding of attack and defense mechanisms in training models sequentially under regularization-based CL, which is vulnerable to data poisoning.
AI.method: The authors model adversary-defender interaction as an online zero-sum game, establishing performance limits under attack scenarios and proposing task verification and robust defense mechanisms for detectable and bounded poisoning cases.
AI.result: The study demonstrates theoretical and experimental validation for defense strategies against infrequent attacks and noise-bound poisoning in CL, showing improved convergence rates.
AI.conclusion: Despite inherent vulnerabilities in regularization-based CL, effective defenses such as task verification and sensitivity minimization can mitigate risks under specific attack regimes.

title: Empirical Evaluation of Multi-Modal Touch Detection in Over-the-Shoulder Video Surveillance
summary: Video Intelligence Surveillance (VIDINT) on over-the-shoulder footage is a proposed vector for monitoring human-computer interaction patterns without direct screen recording access. In this paper, we evaluate a Behavioral Intelligence (BEHINT) touch-detection framework designed to reconstruct keystroke events on mobile keypad interfaces from physical finger interactions. Our system integrates four parallel detection modalities: (1) anatomical hand landmarks via MediaPipe, (2) HSV skin color filtering, (3) temporal frame differencing for motion detection, and (4) shape-guided Canny edge analysis. We map relative touch coordinates to a reference screen layout to reconstruct typing sequences. Evaluation on a 120-frame first-person staged video of passcode entry reveals that while MediaPipe and Skin Detection fail to run autonomously due to partial hand occlusion and ambient noise, Motion-Only and Edge-Only configurations achieve F1-scores of 18.5% and 18.2%, respectively. The combined multi-modal configuration achieves an F1-score of 16.7% and a sequence similarity of 3.0% when mapped to the iOS passcode layout. We conduct ablation, resolution decay, noise sensitivity, and proximity threshold tuning to characterize the system's operational envelope. We then audit generalization on 5 real, publicly licensed third-person phone videos and find that the detector emits a median of 57 touch points per frame (peaking at 205), one to three orders of magnitude more than the rate of real taps, because the skin filter responds to the whole hand rather than to fingertip contact. The staged keystroke result does not survive contact with uncontrolled footage; the system does not achieve reliable keystroke reconstruction outside the calibrated staged setting.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates a framework for detecting touch interactions on mobile devices from video footage, leveraging four detection methods, but finds limited accuracy and scalability.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to explore a video surveillance method for monitoring typing interactions on mobile devices without direct screen access, advancing non-intrusive human-computer interaction analytics.
AI.method: The proposed BEHINT touch-detection system employs four detection modalities: anatomical landmarks, skin color filtering, motion analysis, and edge detection, and maps detected touch coordinates to a keyboard layout.
AI.result: The system shows limited effectiveness, achieving an F1-score below 20% in controlled conditions and failing to generalize to real-world video scenarios, with high false detection rates.
AI.conclusion: While innovative in approach, the system is unreliable in practical settings due to poor generalization, noise sensitivity, and limited performance outside a staged environment.

title: Comparing Chatbot Performance Enhanced with Persistent Homology
summary: Chatbots have become increasingly prevalent across various domains, offering automated assistance in many areas, especially mental health support. The training is done using extremely large datasets, which are sometimes not available in very specific domains. Moreover, it would sometimes be ideal to train the chatbot with personal information about the patients, which, of course, cannot be done on shared servers since it would violate patient confidentiality. Hence, being able to improve the performance of a chatbot, possibly trained locally and on a restricted dataset, without having to increase the dataset itself, would be extremely beneficial. In this work, we will enhance the input datasets using persistent homology (PH) vectorizations computed from the raw datasets themselves. Then we will compare, across several metrics, the performance of multiple chatbot models with or without the PH enhancement. Our experiments suggest that, while at times the PH enhancement is not particularly beneficial, it sometimes brings remarkable advantages for virtually no cost.

AI.tldr: This paper explores improving chatbot performance in specific domains with limited data by using persistent homology (PH) enhancements to augment input datasets.
AI.motivation: Limited availability of large datasets in specific domains and the need for privacy-preserved chatbot training make it challenging to develop effective models capable of providing specialized support, like mental health assistance.
AI.method: The authors propose enhancing input datasets with persistent homology (PH) vectorizations derived from raw data. They assess multiple chatbot models' performance with and without this enhancement across various metrics.
AI.result: The experiments reveal that PH enhancements can occasionally provide remarkable performance improvements to chatbot training, even though they are not universally beneficial.
AI.conclusion: While PH-enhanced datasets are not always advantageous, they offer significant benefits in some cases with minimal computational cost, making this approach a promising direction for improving specialized chatbots.

title: Benchmark AUC Is Not Deployable Reliability: A Cross-Dataset Audit of Off-the-Shelf Features for Surveillance Video Anomaly Detection
summary: Automated "suspicious behavior" flagging is a headline promise of AI surveillance, and the field reports high frame-level ROC-AUC on standard video anomaly detection benchmarks. Those numbers are measured by training and testing on the same camera and scene. We audit what happens when that assumption is dropped. We build an unsupervised normality model from the all-normal training frames of one dataset, using frozen off-the-shelf embeddings (CLIP, DINOv2, ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B0) and a nearest-neighbour distance, and score the test frames of the same and of other datasets. Across 4 real datasets (UCSD Ped1, UCSD Ped2, CUHK Avenue, ShanghaiTech) and 4 backbones, same-dataset AUC averages 0.704 but cross-dataset AUC averages 0.499, which is chance: a detector calibrated on one scene is no better than a coin flip on another, and in several pairs it is below chance. The strongest backbone makes this worse, not better: DINOv2 has the best same-dataset AUC (up to 0.901 on Ped2) and the largest cross-dataset drop. The collapse is not an artefact of the scoring rule: replacing the nearest-neighbour detector with a PaDiM-style Mahalanobis detector reproduces it almost exactly (cross-dataset gap 0.202 versus 0.208). Even at a favourable operating point the false-alarm rate is on the order of 31,931 per hour. We conclude that the benchmark numbers quoted for surveillance anomaly detection describe a calibrated laboratory setting and overstate deployable reliability by a wide margin, and we release the code that reproduces every number.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the limitations of current AI surveillance systems by showing their poor performance in cross-dataset scenarios, despite success within the same dataset, and highlights the need for more realistic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To assess whether AI surveillance systems' high performance in detecting anomalies in video datasets translates to real-world applications, especially across different scenes or camera settings.
AI.method: Constructed unsupervised normality models using different neural network embeddings (e.g., CLIP, DINOv2) and evaluated anomaly detection on both same-dataset and cross-dataset settings using nearest-neighbour and Mahalanobis detectors.
AI.result: While same-dataset AUC averages 0.704, cross-dataset AUC drops to 0.499 (chance level), highlighting the systems' inability to generalize. DINOv2, despite being the strongest backbone, showed the largest performance drop across datasets.
AI.conclusion: Existing benchmarks for video anomaly detection are overly optimistic and fail to reflect real-world deployability, needing more realistic and rigorous testing protocols. The code for reproducing results is made available by the authors.

title: Agentic Safety is an Epistemic Property, Not a Behavioral One
comment: To appear in proceedings of ICML 2026
summary: Contemporary AI safety spans pre-training interventions, post-training alignment, deployment-time controls, monitoring, and red-teaming. These methods are necessary, but they primarily certify snapshots of system behavior. As AI systems become more capable, dynamic, embodied, and self-improving, this snapshot view becomes incomplete: safety depends not only on whether a system behaves acceptably now, but whether it remains correctable as it learns, adapts, acts, and modifies itself over time. This paper argues that safety should therefore be treated as an epistemic property of the evolving learner, not merely a behavioral property of the current policy. We introduce teachability as the capacity to preserve future corrective leverage under bounded human, institutional, or environmental intervention. We argue that advanced systems can retain visible competence while eroding the representational, algorithmic, or meta-decision conditions needed for future correction. Safe advanced AI systems must not only behave acceptably now; they must remain teachable later.

AI.tldr: This paper emphasizes that AI safety should focus on ensuring systems remain correctable and teachable over time, not just evaluating their current behavior.
AI.motivation: Highlight the insufficiency of current AI safety measures that focus only on snapshot evaluations, considering the dynamic, adaptive, and self-improving nature of advanced AI systems.
AI.method: Introduce the concept of "teachability," which refers to the ability to retain corrective leverage as systems change and evolve, ensuring future interventions remain effective.
AI.result: The notion that advanced AI systems must retain conditions for future corrections was theoretically discussed, showing how safety depends on preserving mechanisms for teachability.
AI.conclusion: AI safety should go beyond assessing present behavior and integrate strategies to maintain systems' teachability over time.

title: RoAd-RL: A Unified Library and Benchmark for Robust Adversarial Reinforcement Learning
summary:
Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has achieved significant success in robotics and autonomous systems, yet remains vulnerable to adversarial perturbations that can severely degrade performance. Research in adversarial reinforcement learning is often limited by fragmented implementations, inconsistent evaluation protocols, and poor reproducibility. To address these challenges, we present \textbf{RoAd-RL}, an open-source benchmarking framework that provides unified abstractions for policies, attacks, defenses, and robustness metrics, together with reproducible evaluation pipelines and seamless integration with Stable-Baselines3 and Gymnasium.
  We evaluate DQN, PPO, and SAC agents in LunarLander and Highway-v0 under 192 attack-defense configurations. Results reveal substantial variations in robustness across environments and show that some commonly used defenses can be more detrimental than the attacks they aim to mitigate, while temporal smoothing consistently achieves strong performance. RoAd-RL establishes a standardized benchmark for adversarial reinforcement learning research and is publicly available at https://pypi.org/project/road-rl.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RoAd-RL, an open-source benchmarking framework for adversarial reinforcement learning research, addressing issues of fragmented implementations and reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Current research in adversarial reinforcement learning suffers from fragmented implementations, inconsistent evaluation methods, and low reproducibility.
AI.method: The authors present RoAd-RL, which provides unified abstractions for policies, attacks, defenses, and metrics, along with reproducible evaluation pipelines integrated with popular frameworks like Stable-Baselines3 and Gymnasium.
AI.result: Evaluations of common DRL agents (DQN, PPO, and SAC) on specific environments demonstrate significant robustness variations and show that some defenses can be counterproductive, while temporal smoothing is a consistently effective defense.
AI.conclusion: RoAd-RL standardizes adversarial reinforcement learning benchmarks, enabling reproducible and comprehensive evaluation. The framework is openly available for public use.

title: Scenes as Objects, Not Primitives: Instance-Structured 3D Tokenization from Unposed Views
summary: A 3D scene is understood through its objects, not the primitives that compose them. Yet feed-forward reconstruction methods output dense, unstructured sets of points or Gaussians, leaving object-level structure to be recovered after the fact. We propose a feed-forward framework that decomposes a scene into instance-structured 3D token groups directly from unposed multi-view images -- compact object-centric units from which reconstruction, segmentation, and manipulation all follow. Each token group pairs an instance token capturing entity-level identity with anchor tokens that encode local geometry and appearance, which are decoded into a set of 3D Gaussians. This two-level factorization decouples object identity from local appearance, making object instances a native interface of the representation rather than a derived product. The token groups are learned through differentiable rendering with joint reconstruction and segmentation supervision, requiring no 3D annotations. Our feed-forward model surpasses per-scene optimization baselines in class-agnostic instance segmentation while remaining competitive in novel view synthesis. Beyond these metrics, the same token groups directly unlock instance-level scene editing -- removing, translating, or inserting objects by operating on their groups -- as well as efficient open-vocabulary 3D instance retrieval, where retrieval complexity scales with the number of instances rather than primitives.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a feed-forward method to decompose 3D scenes into object-centric, structured representations directly from multi-view images, enabling instance-based applications.
AI.motivation: Current 3D reconstruction methods lack direct object-level decomposition, requiring post-processing to recover structure. This limits their ability to natively support segmentation, editing, or manipulation.
AI.method: The authors propose a feed-forward framework that creates instance-structured 3D token groups, combining instance tokens for identity with anchor tokens for local geometry and appearance, learned through differentiable rendering and without requiring 3D annotations.
AI.result: The proposed model outperforms optimization-based baselines in class-agnostic instance segmentation and maintains competitive performance in novel view synthesis. It also supports new applications like instance-level 3D scene editing and efficient open-vocabulary object retrieval.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that representing scenes as object-structured token groups offers significant advantages in segmentation, reconstruction, and interaction, making object instances a core part of the representation.

title: HMARS: A Hierarchical Multi-Agent Memory System for Long-Context Reasoning
summary: Long-context reasoning requires models to access, retrieve, and integrate evidence scattered across documents, dialogues, and accumulated interaction histories. Standard retrieval-augmented generation reduces this problem to top-$K$ chunk retrieval, but such passive access can discard relevant evidence before reasoning begins, especially when relevance depends on broader context. We propose HMARS, a hierarchical multi-agent memory system that treats long contexts as managed memory rather than a flat retrieval corpus. Sub-agents maintain grounded access to bounded memory regions, mid-agents manage regional context and provide query-specific coordination, and a frontier model performs final reasoning over retrieved evidence pages. To evaluate this view, we construct two diagnostic benchmarks targeting evidence breadth and context-dependent relevance. Across long-document and multi-turn memory tasks, HMARS achieves the best overall performance against retrieval, reranking, full-context, graph-based, and agentic long-context baselines. Evidence coverage analysis further shows that its gains come from retrieving the required supporting evidence more completely, rather than merely changing the final answer prompt.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HMARS, a hierarchical multi-agent memory system for long-context reasoning, which improves evidence retrieval and reasoning compared to baseline methods.
AI.motivation: To address limitations in standard retrieval-augmented generation that struggles to maintain relevant evidence across scattered and context-dependent long documents or interactions.
AI.method: HMARS uses a hierarchical approach with sub-agents managing bounded memory regions, mid-agents coordinating regional contexts, and a frontier model performing final reasoning over retrieved evidence.
AI.result: HMARS outperforms several baseline methods in diagnostic benchmarks for evidence breadth and context-relevance, showing improved complete evidence retrieval for long-context tasks.
AI.conclusion: HMARS improves long-context reasoning by hierarchically organizing memory access and ensuring better evidence coverage, providing a step forward in retrieval-augmented reasoning systems.

title: MotionAtlas: Detailed Region Captioning for Motion-Centric Videos
summary: We propose MotionAtlas, a system for detailed captioning of motion-centric videos, comprising (1) a dedicated human-annotated benchmark, (2) a scalable, high-quality pipeline to construct training samples, and (3) a family of powerful Video-MLLMs. Unlike conventional global motion captioning datasets, we focus on region-aware motion captioning: given a video and a spatiotemporal mask, the model generates precise descriptions of motion within the target region, thereby alleviating visual clutter and motion entanglement and enabling reliable, quantifiable evaluation. Concretely, we first build MotionAtlas-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark comprising 2,073 multiple-choice questions, meticulously annotated for a curated set of high-quality, motion-centric videos, to evaluate fine-grained motion understanding of the objects in question. Second, we design a rigorous and scalable data pipeline that leverages self-bootstrap refinement to suppress fine-grained hallucinations, yielding 159k high-quality motion captioning data. Third, we design a tailored training data composition strategy, which achieves consistent and substantial performance gains across diverse baseline Video-MLLMs, including Molmo2 and Qwen3-VL. For instance, MotionAtlas-4B surpasses Qwen3-VL-4B by an average of 5.2 percentage points across general motion benchmarks. The benchmark, dataset, and code have been released.

AI.tldr: MotionAtlas introduces a specialized system for detailed, region-aware motion video captioning through a benchmark, data pipeline, and improved Video-MLLMs.
AI.motivation: Current motion captioning systems struggle with visual clutter and motion entanglement, making it difficult to analyze specific motions within a video.
AI.method: The paper establishes a benchmark (MotionAtlas-Bench), a data pipeline for refining high-quality training data, and proposes a data composition strategy to enhance diverse Video-MLLM baselines.
AI.result: MotionAtlas-4B achieves an average 5.2 percentage point improvement over Qwen3-VL-4B across general motion benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The introduced system enables precise motion description in videos, overcomes existing challenges, and advances baseline Video-MLLM capabilities. All resources are open-sourced to foster further development.

title: From Regulatory Approvals to Patents: Cross-Domain Linking for Cardiovascular Device Traceability
summary: Linking FDA-approved medical devices to their underlying United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) patents enables critical applications such as recall root-cause analysis, M&A-driven IP discovery, and technology trajectory mapping. However, this cross-domain entity linking task remains unexplored due to severe *semantic gaps*: FDA documents focus on clinical outcomes, while patents describe technical mechanisms, yielding minimal lexical overlap. We formalize medical device-patent linking as a challenging cross-domain entity linking problem characterized by label scarcity and domain shifts. Using cardiovascular devices as a high-impact, representative domain featuring diverse technologies, high recall rates, and abundant disclosures, we construct a benchmark with 434 devices, 698K patents, and 585 high-fidelity expert-verified pairs. To address these challenges, we propose Bridge-MedDevKG, a coarse-to-fine framework that integrates (1) **MedDevOnto**, a domain-specific ontology that anchors device concepts via three-tier UMLS normalization; (2) **Multi-signal candidate generation** fusing company affiliation, semantic similarity, and ontology-weighted entity overlap; and (3) **Heterogeneous reranking** with multi-signal scoring and XGBoost classification on hard negatives. Our approach achieves a conservative lower-bound recall of 91.6% on the gold standard with 50.9% noise reduction, substantially outperforming LLM baselines under comparable evaluation. The resulting MedDevKG provides 6.8M high-confidence links, laying a scalable foundation for regulatory-IP integration across medical specialties.

AI.tldr: The paper develops Bridge-MedDevKG, a cross-domain entity linking framework to connect FDA-approved medical devices with USPTO patents. It focuses on overcoming semantic gaps and label scarcity for applications like recall analysis and IP discovery.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the cross-domain entity linking challenge between FDA medical devices and USPTO patents to enable applications such as root-cause analysis of recalls, technological tracking, and IP integration across medical domains.
AI.method: The method includes a framework integrating MedDevOnto ontology for device concept anchoring, multi-signal candidate generation leveraging affiliation and semantic signals, and heterogeneous reranking using XGBoost classifiers.
AI.result: Bridge-MedDevKG achieves 91.6% recall on expert-verified data with 50.9% noise reduction, surpassing large language model baselines. It creates a database of 6.8M high-confidence device-patent links.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a significant advancement in linking medical devices to patents, serving as a scalable foundation for future applications in regulatory and intellectual property analysis.

title: A Good Talk Does not Look Like a Summary, It Teaches You! Measuring Takeaways from Paper-to-Video Talks
summary: Automatically generated videos from scientific papers are increasingly used for education and research dissemination. However, existing evaluation metrics mainly measure visual quality or whether key points from the paper appear in the video without assessing whether the video actually helps viewers understand the ideas. We introduce EffectivePresentationScorer, a framework for evaluating the instructional quality of scientific presentation videos. It checks whether a video explains the main ideas clearly, introduces needed background concepts, and connects technical details to the main contribution of the paper. When we apply EffectivePresentationScorer to the existing paper-to-video generation systems, we find that generated videos mention the correct topics and follow the structure of the paper but fail to explain prerequisite concepts or clarify why the method works. These failures are often ignored by existing video evaluation metrics, which focus on content presence rather than explanatory quality.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new framework, EffectivePresentationScorer, to evaluate scientific presentation videos based on their instructional quality, rather than solely visual or content-based metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation metrics for paper-to-video systems focus on visual and content aspects but fail to assess whether videos enhance viewer understanding.
AI.method: EffectivePresentationScorer evaluates videos by checking clarity in explanations, coverage of background concepts, and connections between technical details and main contributions.
AI.result: Findings show paper-to-video systems produce videos with correct topics and structure but often fail to clarify prerequisite concepts and underlying mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Current evaluation metrics miss critical aspects of explanatory quality, highlighting the need for a more rigorous assessment framework like EffectivePresentationScorer.

title: Same Concept, Different Directions: Cross-Modal Feature Heterogeneity in Sparse Autoencoders
summary: Vision-language models map images and text into a joint embedding space. However, these embeddings often entangle multiple semantic features, which limits their interpretability and controllability. While sparse autoencoders have emerged as a useful tool for decomposing these embeddings into monosemantic features, their application to joint embedding spaces has largely relied on an implicit, untested assumption that semantically corresponding features share the same directions across modalities. In this paper, we challenge this assumption by identifying discrepancies in feature directions for the same concept across image and text modalities, a phenomenon we term cross-modal feature heterogeneity. We demonstrate that this heterogeneity is a key driver of the modality split, where a shared concept activates different latents depending on the modality. This finding further reveals why aligning latent activations alone is insufficient to resolve the underlying feature mismatch. Motivated by this observation, we propose an approach that trains modality-specific sparse autoencoders to preserve each modality's feature geometry, and then aligns corresponding features post hoc. Our method improves reconstruction fidelity and enhances performance in cross-modal retrieval and concept steering.

AI.tldr: This paper studies cross-modal feature heterogeneity in vision-language models and proposes a new method to address it using modality-specific sparse autoencoders with a post hoc alignment approach.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of interpretability and controllability in vision-language model embeddings by understanding and resolving cross-modal feature discrepancies.
AI.method: The paper introduces modality-specific sparse autoencoders for preserving each modality's feature geometry, followed by post hoc alignment of corresponding features.
AI.result: The proposed method improves reconstruction fidelity and outperforms in tasks like cross-modal retrieval and concept steering.
AI.conclusion: Feature heterogeneity across modalities in joint embedding spaces can significantly impact performance, and the proposed approach effectively resolves this mismatch, leading to enhanced usability of vision-language models.

title: GarmentZoom: Generating Zoomable Images from Garment Listings
summary: Online product listings for garments often include an overview photo and a close-up to show garment details. However, each photo focuses on either field of view or garment detail, forcing users to alternate between views and breaking browsing continuity. We present GarmentZoom, a system that enhances the full-view photo to match the fidelity of its accompanying close-up, enabling seamless zoom-and-pan exploration. Unlike standard reference-based super-resolution, our setting involves close-up references that are spatially unaligned with the full view, and scale factors that vary substantially across garments 3-20$\times$. Prior work typically relies on alignment to transfer details or requires per-instance fine-tuning to memorize them. Instead, we train a single model that supports a continuous range of scales across diverse garments. Our approach synthesizes details without requiring spatial alignment and matches the quality of per-instance methods with a fraction of the training cost.

AI.tldr: GarmentZoom introduces a system to enhance full-view garment photos using close-up details. The system enables seamless zoom-and-pan exploration while addressing challenges such as spatial unalignment and varying scale factors.
AI.motivation: Browsing garment product listings is disrupted by alternating between overview photos and close-up details due to focus differences. Solving this issue could improve user experience and browsing continuity.
AI.method: A single model is trained to synthesize garment details across varying scales and diverse garments without requiring spatial alignment, as opposed to prior fine-tuning or alignment-dependent methods.
AI.result: The system achieves detail synthesis quality on par with per-instance methods, while significantly reducing training costs.
AI.conclusion: GarmentZoom provides a seamless exploration experience by matching full-view photo fidelity with close-up details, overcoming technical challenges efficiently and effectively.

title: SafeGEO: Understanding Generative Engine Optimization Risks in Recommendation Agents
summary: Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) lets content owners rewrite web content to increase their visibility in generative systems. In recommendation agents, this creates a risk that seller-controlled sources make flawed products appear better supported than they are. We study this risk by asking whether recommendation agents preserve utility-aligned decisions when seller-controlled sources are rewritten for GEO. To make this question measurable, we construct SafeGEO, an evaluation suite with 22 GEO attack variants across 600 recommendation cases. We empirically show that GEO attacks can promote flawed target products. On average, they increase the rate at which such flawed products enter the recommendation set by up to 83.2%. We further study whether agent-side design choices can mitigate this risk and show that simple defenses, including defensive prompting and structured evidence checks, reduce harmful target promotion by up to 39.2%. These gains are substantial but do not restore the no-GEO performance, showing that GEO remains a serious risk despite developer-side mitigation.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) poses risks in recommendation systems by promoting flawed products, showing a sharp increase in harmful target promotion and proposing partial mitigations.
AI.motivation: To assess whether recommendation agents can maintain utility-aligned decisions when seller-controlled content is rewritten via GEO.
AI.method: The authors use SafeGEO, an evaluation suite with 22 attack variants across 600 recommendation cases, to measure how GEO attacks influence recommendation systems.
AI.result: GEO attacks increase flawed product promotion by up to 83.2%, while defensive measures like prompting and evidence checks reduce harm by up to 39.2%, but don't fully mitigate it.
AI.conclusion: GEO poses a serious risk to recommendation systems even with partial mitigations in place, calling for further efforts in safeguarding content recommendations.

title: Who Plays Which Role When? Communication Role Dynamics for Peer Recognition and Team Performance Prediction
summary: Team roles offer an interpretable lens on collaboration, yet computational studies of roles often rely on domain-specific personas or data-driven clustering rather than theory-grounded taxonomies. We operationalize a taxonomy of eight communication roles grounded in education literature and annotate a corpus of 6,307 Slack messages from 55 students across 18 teams in a semester-long computer science course project. We evaluate whether LLMs can approximate expert labels, enabling scalable, taxonomy-driven role annotation. Using these role labels, we characterize role dynamics over teams' lifecycles, finding that different roles peak at different moments and that students enact a more diverse set of roles as projects progress. To evaluate the utility of our role constructs, we use them to predict peer recognition, outperforming lexical, conversational, and LLM-prompting baselines. To assess generalizability beyond the educational context, we apply the same role constructs to a public dataset (DeliData) to predict team performance improvement after deliberation, again exceeding prior performance.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a taxonomy of communication roles based on educational literature, evaluates its applicability with LLMs, and demonstrates its effectiveness in predicting peer recognition and team performance improvement.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to provide a theory-grounded approach to studying team roles, addressing limitations in computational studies that rely on either domain-specific or purely data-driven methods.
AI.method: The authors introduce an eight-role taxonomy rooted in education literature, annotate Slack messages of students, and evaluate large language models (LLMs) for role labeling. They assess the roles' utility on educational and non-educational datasets.
AI.result: Findings show that LLMs can approximate expert role annotations, roles evolve dynamically during project lifecycles, and the constructs predict peer recognition and team performance better than alternative approaches.
AI.conclusion: The taxonomy facilitates scalable role annotation, provides insights into role dynamics, and proves to be effective across contexts, suggesting broad applicability for understanding and improving team collaboration.

title: Golden Hour Divide: Trauma Care Accessibility and Resource Vulnerability in Sri Lanka
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures. Accepted for presentation at MERCon 2026. Preprint version
summary: Timely intensive care dictates survival, yet emergency infrastructure remains unevenly distributed across Sri Lanka. While pre-hospital services have expanded, the transition to definitive care remains a critical bottleneck. This study evaluates national emergency resilience by quantifying the gap between clinical demand and the availability of specialized resources across all 25 districts. Using the latest national epidemiological data and terrain-aware H3 hexagonal modeling, we analyzed accessibility for seven critical conditions based on spatial gaps, clinical need-gaps, lethality, coverage, and resource availability. Based on these metrics, unsupervised K-Means clustering was applied to categorize districts into four policy-actionable archetypes: Critical Structural Exclusion, Institutional Mirages, Operational Capacity Strain, and High-Resilience Benchmarks. Our study suggests that severe service deficits exist in the Northern and Eastern provinces, where spatial gaps exceed 70%, rendering the Golden Hour operationally impossible. Notably, specialist scarcity drives systemic pressure more than bed capacity; underserved regions effectively function as institutional mirages. This study suggests that improving accessibility by 25% in high-priority clusters would reduce the national need-gap by 9.65%, providing a roadmap for the strategic redistribution of specialists to ensure healthcare equity.

AI.tldr: This paper assesses the healthcare infrastructure in Sri Lanka for critical care, identifying accessibility gaps and suggesting strategies for equitable resource distribution.
AI.motivation: To evaluate the mismatch between the increasing clinical demand for critical care and the uneven distribution of emergency healthcare resources in Sri Lanka.
AI.method: The study used national epidemiological data, terrain-aware H3 hexagonal spatial modeling, and seven critical condition metrics. It employed unsupervised K-Means clustering to classify districts into actionable archetypes of healthcare accessibility and resilience.
AI.result: The analysis revealed severe service deficits in the Northern and Eastern provinces, with accessibility gaps exceeding 70%. Specialist shortages are a larger issue than bed scarcity. Strategic improvements could reduce the national healthcare gap significantly.
AI.conclusion: A 25% improvement in accessibility within critical regions could close a significant healthcare gap, highlighting the need for strategic redistribution of specialists to achieve equity in emergency care across Sri Lanka.

title: Reliability-Prioritized Fine-Grained Generation in Multimodal Large
comment: Equal contribution: Xiaomeng Fan and Wu Wei. Corresponding authors: Zhi Gao and Yunde Jia
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly expected to generate fine-grained descriptions of visual content. However, we observe and theoretically show that generating fine-grained responses poses a reliability challenge, \textit{i.e.}, fine-grained generation is more error-prone than coarse-grained generation. This phenomenon suggests that models should generate the finest description that remains reliable rather than simply produce more specific outputs. To investigate this problem, we develop \textsc{GranFact}, a granularity-aware benchmark consisting of expert-verified multi-object images with coarse-to-fine category annotations. Then, we design a hierarchy-aware evaluation algorithm, which assesses both whether model predictions are visually correct and how specific the correct predictions are. We also propose a reliability-prioritized preference optimization method based on Direct Preference Optimization, which penalizes unreliable fine-grained claims while rewarding reliable specificity. Experiments on \textsc{GranFact} show that our method improves fine-grained generation while preserving reliability. Code and data are available \href{https://github.com/WeiWu2025/GranFact}{here}.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge of reliability in fine-grained visual descriptions generated by multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and introduces a benchmark (GranFact) and evaluation method to improve such generation.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this research is the observed tendency of fine-grained responses in MLLMs to be more error-prone than coarse-grained ones, leading to the need for a method that balances specificity and reliability.
AI.method: The authors developed the GranFact benchmark with multi-object images annotated across granularity levels and designed a hierarchy-aware evaluation algorithm, as well as a novel reliability-focused optimization approach based on Direct Preference Optimization.
AI.result: The proposed method demonstrated improved accuracy in fine-grained visual descriptions while maintaining reliability when tested on the GranFact benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Balancing specificity with reliability in MLLM-generated visual descriptions is critical, and the introduced methods effectively enhance this balance, paving the way for more dependable fine-grained outputs.

title: ReasonRec: A Reasoning-Augmented Multimodal Agent for Unified Recommendation
comment: The 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026)
summary: Recent advances in multimodal recommenders excel at feature fusion but remain opaque and inefficient decision-makers, lacking explicit reasoning and self-awareness of uncertainty. We introduce ReasonRec, a reasoning-augmented multimodal agent structured around a three-stage explicit reasoning pipeline. Specifically, we propose a reasoning-aware visual instruction tuning strategy that systematically transforms diverse recommendation tasks into unified CoT prompts, enabling the VLM to explicitly articulate intermediate decision steps. Additionally, our evidence-horizon curriculum progressively enhances the reasoning complexity to better handle cold-start and long-tail user scenarios, significantly boosting model generalization. Furthermore, the uncertainty-guided delegation mechanism empowers the agent to assess its own confidence, strategically allocating computational resources to optimize both recommendation accuracy and inference efficiency. Comprehensive experiments on four standard recommendation tasks across five real-world datasets demonstrate that ReasonRec achieves over 30% relative improvement in key ranking metrics compared to state-of-the-art multimodal recommenders. Crucially, ReasonRec substantially reduces inference latency by dynamically delegating up to 35% of queries to efficient sub-models without compromising accuracy. Extensive ablation studies further confirm that each proposed reasoning and planning mechanism individually contributes substantially to ReasonRec's overall effectiveness. Collectively, our results illustrate a clear pathway towards interpretable, adaptive, and efficient multimodal recommendation through explicit reasoning and agentic design.

AI.tldr: ReasonRec introduces a reasoning-augmented multimodal recommendation system with explicit reasoning pipelines, achieving high efficiency and interpretability.
AI.motivation: To address the opaqueness and inefficiency in decision-making of current multimodal recommenders, along with their lack of explicit reasoning and uncertainty awareness.
AI.method: The paper proposes ReasonRec, featuring a three-stage reasoning pipeline with visual instruction tuning, an evidence-horizon curriculum for better generalization, and an uncertainty-guided delegation mechanism for computational efficiency.
AI.result: ReasonRec improves key recommendation metrics by over 30% relative to state-of-the-art models, reduces inference latency by dynamically delegating up to 35% of queries, and maintains accuracy.
AI.conclusion: ReasonRec demonstrates that explicit reasoning and agentic design lead to interpretable, adaptive, and efficient multimodal recommendation systems, showcasing significant improvements in both performance and computational efficiency.

title: CW-B: Class Weighted Boosting Framework for Imbalance Resilient Multi Class Cardiac Phenotyping
summary: Cardiac discharge phenotyping informs post-discharge treatment and follow-up, but real-world records are often incomplete and class-imbalanced, increasing the risk of missed high-risk phenotypes. We propose CW-B, a clinical risk-aligned class-weighted XGBoost pipeline for five-class cardiac discharge phenotyping under real-world class imbalance and missingness. CW-B combines fold-specific class-balanced instance weighting, missingness-indicator augmentation, and classwise error auditing to improve recognition of clinically prioritized phenotypes while preserving interpretable and auditable decision logic. In five-fold stratified cross-validation, CW-B achieves the best Accuracy, Macro-F1, Balanced Accuracy, and Prioritized F1 among tree-based, ensemble, and neural baselines. Overall, CW-B provides a practical and deployment-oriented approach for more reliable cardiac discharge phenotyping in real-world clinical settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CW-B, an XGBoost-based pipeline for accurate and interpretable cardiac discharge phenotyping under class imbalance and missing data conditions.
AI.motivation: Classifying cardiac discharge phenotypes is critical for post-discharge care and treatment, but current methods struggle with imbalanced and incomplete real-world clinical data.
AI.method: The authors propose the CW-B pipeline, which includes class-balanced weighting, missingness indicators, and error auditing within the XGBoost framework to address data challenges and prioritize clinical relevance in predictions.
AI.result: Through five-fold cross-validation, CW-B outperformed various baselines, achieving high performance metrics like Accuracy, Macro-F1, and Balanced Accuracy, even under challenging clinical data conditions.
AI.conclusion: CW-B offers a practical, interpretable, and effective solution for cardiac discharge phenotyping in clinical applications, improving reliability and deployment readiness.

title: ReMAP-PET: Beyond Visual Understanding -- Learning Region-Guided Metabolic Alignment Semantics from Brain PET
summary: Positron Emission Tomography (PET) reveals brain metabolism and is clinically central to neurodegenerative disease assessment, yet existing 3D brain foundation models treat PET as generic volumetric data, missing the structured regional metabolic information that distinguishes it from structural neuroimaging. To address these limitations, we propose ReMAP-PET, a framework that moves beyond visual encoding by supervising a partially-tuned MedicalNet 3D ResNet-50 with brain regional standardized uptake value ratio (SUVR) profiles through joint regression and contrastive objectives, enabling the encoder to learn the metabolic semantics underlying PET modality. On 1015 paired PET--SUVR samples, ReMAP-PET achieves 0.070 SUVR MAE and 77.8% PET SUVR Recall@1, substantially outperforming five frozen pretrained baselines. We further connect the metabolic embedding to clinical language via contrastive alignment with frozen BioClinicalBERT and demonstrate end-to-end PET-to-report generation through SUVR-constrained verbalization. Linear probing on diagnostic classification and cognitive regression tasks confirms that the embeddings retain clinically relevant information without task-specific fine-tuning. Our results show that grounding PET encoders in regional metabolic semantics -- rather than treating PET as generic volumetric data -- yields representations that are structured, interpretable, and language-compatible, pointing to a new direction for metabolic-aware PET understanding.

AI.tldr: ReMAP-PET enhances PET analysis by grounding brain metabolism in structured regional semantics, outperforming existing methods and aligning PET data with clinical language for advanced diagnostics.
AI.motivation: Current 3D brain foundation models treat PET data as generic volumetric information, ignoring the structured metabolic information vital for neurodegenerative disease assessment.
AI.method: ReMAP-PET uses a partially-tuned MedicalNet 3D ResNet-50 supervised by brain regional standardized uptake value ratio (SUVR) profiles through joint regression and contrastive learning objectives.
AI.result: ReMAP-PET demonstrated superior performance in SUVR prediction (0.070 MAE, 77.8% Recall@1) over five baseline models, aligning metabolic embeddings with clinical context and enabling PET-to-report generation while retaining clinically relevant information.
AI.conclusion: ReMAP-PET leverages regional metabolic semantics for interpretable, structured, and language-compatible PET representations, advancing metabolic-aware PET understanding for clinical use.

title: EVAF: A Test-Retest Protocol for Selective Parametric Consolidation
summary: Long-running language agents need mechanisms for deciding which experiences should persist after the working context is gone. Retrieval systems can reinsert past text, but they do not by themselves show that an experience has been selectively consolidated into the model's own behavior. We introduce EVAF, an Echo-Valence Attractor Field mechanism for gated LoRA consolidation, and a test-retest protocol for measuring selective parametric consolidation under controlled interference. Across GPT-2 and TinyLlama, EVAF preferentially consolidates high-valence, high-surprise experiences while preserving retrieval-accessible factual memory through a complementary routed memory path. Test-retest measurements show stronger post-interference behavioral persistence than frozen, retrieval-only, and ungated continual-update baselines, while keeping parameter drift and cross-persona contamination low. The results support a separation between memory access and memory depth: retrieving a fact and internalizing an experience are distinct computational operations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EVAF, a mechanism for selectively consolidating experiences into AI models, addressing the need for persistent memory in language agents.
AI.motivation: To enable long-running language agents to selectively retain important experiences beyond their immediate working context, addressing limitations in retrieval-only systems.
AI.method: The authors propose EVAF, an Echo-Valence Attractor Field mechanism using gated LoRA consolidation, and employ a test-retest protocol to measure consolidation effectiveness under interference.
AI.result: EVAF preferentially consolidates high-valence and high-surprise experiences, maintains factual memory accessibility, and outperforms baseline methods in post-interference behavioral persistence while minimizing parameter drift and cross-persona contamination.
AI.conclusion: Retrieving facts and internalizing experiences are distinct operations, and EVAF effectively separates memory access from memory depth, enabling more robust experience consolidation in AI systems.

title: ScAle: Attention Head Scaling as a Minimal Adapter for Spatial Reasoning in Vision Language Models
summary: Spatial reasoning remains a persistent challenge for many vision language models (VLMs), and improving it typically requires fine-tuning with substantial additional parameters. Our preliminary analysis reveals that rescaling activations in selected transformer layers-without modifying pretrained weights-can significantly influence downstream performance. Motivated by this observation, we propose ScAle, an ultra-lightweight adaptation method that learns a small set of scalar coefficients to modulate last-token attention and MLP activations in a fully frozen backbone. We evaluate our method on the synthetic spatial reasoning benchmark SpatialEval and on real-world VQA datasets (COCOQA and VGQA) across multiple model families. Our method, ScAle, achieves up to 134.1% relative accuracy gains using only 1K trainable parameters without requiring millions of trainable parameters as in standard PEFT methods such as LoRA. Despite its extreme compactness, our approach recovers a substantial fraction of standard PEFT performance while preserving strong non-spatial VQA accuracy. These results demonstrate that bounded activation reweighting provides a simple, architecture-agnostic, and highly parameter-efficient alternative for adapting pretrained VLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "ScAle," a lightweight adaptation method that adjusts activation scalars in frozen vision language models (VLMs) to improve spatial reasoning without requiring large-scale fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: To address the persistent challenge of improving spatial reasoning in VLMs without the need for costly fine-tuning or substantial additional parameters.
AI.method: ScAle modifies pretrained models by learning scalar coefficients for reweighting attention and MLP activations in select transformer layers, without unfreezing the backbone network.
AI.result: ScAle achieved up to 134.1% relative accuracy improvements on spatial reasoning tasks using only 1,000 trainable parameters, outperforming traditional efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA in parameter efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The method demonstrates that small-scale activation reweighting can be an effective, simple, and architecture-agnostic solution for improving VLM spatial reasoning while retaining performance on non-spatial tasks.

title: Carolina Guide: A Multi-Agent RAG System with Institutional Guardrails for Academic Policy Assistance
summary: University students often struggle to navigate complex academic policies, leading to advising bottlenecks and delayed access to critical information. Although large language models (LLMs) offer promise for automated assistance, their tendency toward hallucination and inability to enforce institutional constraints make them unsuitable for high-stakes policy guidance without careful architectural design. We present Carolina Guide, a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system for academic policy assistance at the University of South Carolina (USC). The system employs a modular multi-agent pipeline with institutional guardrails to provide citation-supported, policy-grounded answers to student queries while refusing unsafe requests such as course recommendations or personalized advising. We evaluate the system on a 90 query test set across 6 departments, achieving 98.9% retrieval success at the >= 2 threshold (genuinely relevant results) with the first relevant chunk at rank-1 for 98.9% of queries (MRR at 10 for rel >= 2 = 0.989). Through systematic baseline comparisons and ablation studies, we show that each architectural component-MMR reranking, adequate retrieval context (k=20), and citation enforcement-contributes measurable practical value despite limited statistical power at 90 queries. The evaluation of the guardrail on 30 adversarial queries demonstrates Safety F1 of 0.89, correctly refusing 86% of unsafe queries while maintaining 93% coverage of benign queries. These results show that production-ready LLM systems for institutional policy guidance require rethinking standard RAG patterns to prioritize safety, transparency, and departmental autonomy over conversational sophistication.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Carolina Guide, a system that aids university students by providing accurate academic policy information using a robust retrieval-augmented generation architecture with institutional guardrails.
AI.motivation: Students often face difficulties in accessing clear information about complex academic policies, leading to inefficiencies and bottlenecks in advising processes. This paper aims to address these issues by redefining LLM use for high-stakes scenarios.
AI.method: Carolina Guide is based on a multi-agent RAG pipeline with specific institutional guardrails. It uses mechanisms like MMR re-ranking, citation enforcement, and adequate retrieval context to ensure policy-grounded responses while blocking unsafe queries.
AI.result: The system achieves a high retrieval success rate (98.9%), effective ranking of relevant responses (MRR of 0.989), and demonstrates strong safety measures by refusing unsafe queries (Safety F1=0.89).
AI.conclusion: Production-ready systems for academic policy guidance must prioritize safety and transparency, moving away from standard RAG architectures to address the specific needs of institutions and students.

title: Bandwidth Selection in Kernel Density Estimation for Model Calibration
summary: As deep learning models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes applications, providing well-calibrated uncertainty estimates has become as critical as achieving high predictive accuracy. While Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) has emerged as a smooth and continuous alternative to traditional binning for quantifying miscalibration, its reliability is heavily dependent on the choice of the kernel bandwidth. Standard selection techniques, such as Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), often fail to produce optimal bandwidths for calibration tasks. In this work, we introduce Risk Alignment (RA), a novel optimization framework that determines the optimal bandwidth by aligning KDE-reconstructed risk with empirical risk. We theoretically demonstrate that this alignment minimizes calibration estimation bias across the data distribution, establishing a principled bandwidth selection criterion applicable to various metrics, including the challenging case of canonical calibration error. Extensive experiments across multiple architectures and datasets show that RA consistently outperforms standard bandwidth selection methods, yielding more reliable calibration assessments.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Risk Alignment (RA) framework to optimize kernel bandwidth selection in Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) for better uncertainty calibration in deep learning tasks.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models require well-calibrated uncertainty estimates for reliable deployment in high-stakes applications, but traditional methods for bandwidth selection in KDE often fail in calibration tasks.
AI.method: The paper introduces Risk Alignment (RA), a framework that optimizes bandwidth by aligning KDE-reconstructed risk with empirical risk, thereby minimizing calibration bias.
AI.result: RA is shown to consistently outperform standard bandwidth selection techniques across various architectures and datasets, leading to more reliable calibration assessments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed RA framework provides a principled and practical approach to improving bandwidth selection in KDE, enhancing the reliability of uncertainty calibration in deep learning.

title: SonoCLIP: Mask-Guided Region-Aware Vision-Language Pretraining for Fetal Ultrasound Analysis
summary: Vision-language foundation models have shown strong potential in medical image analysis. Although foundation models for ultrasound imaging have recently emerged, the domain remains particularly challenging due to severe speckle noise, acquisition variability, and subtle anatomical boundaries, leading to high inter-observer variability. Existing CLIP-based models rely primarily on global image-text alignment, limiting their sensitivity to clinically decisive local structures. We propose SonoCLIP, the first million-scale region-controllable fetal ultrasound vision-language foundation model that integrates segmentation masks as mask-channel visual prompts within the vision encoder, enabling joint global-local contrastive representation learning. To support scalable region-text alignment, we introduce a sigmoid-based pairwise contrastive loss that improves stability under large-scale supervision. We further curate a 1.44M-image multimodal fetal ultrasound dataset spanning 24 standard planes for large-scale pretraining. Extensive cross-center evaluations demonstrate that SonoCLIP achieves superior zero-shot transfer performance under both global and mask-guided inference, establishing a controllable and clinically oriented foundation model for fetal ultrasound analysis. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/Harrison-one/SonoCLIP.

AI.tldr: SonoCLIP is a vision-language model designed for fetal ultrasound imaging, introducing segmentation masks and a novel loss function to align global and local features effectively.
AI.motivation: Current models in ultrasound image analysis struggle with noise, variability, and subtle anatomical distinctions, making it critical to improve their focus on localized, clinically relevant features.
AI.method: SonoCLIP integrates segmentation masks as visual prompts and utilizes a novel sigmoid-based pairwise contrastive loss in its vision encoder for coupled global-local representation learning. It is trained on a 1.44M multimodal fetal ultrasound dataset.
AI.result: SonoCLIP demonstrates superior zero-shot transfer performance and achieves robust clinical decision-making capabilities under both global image and localized mask-guided settings.
AI.conclusion: SonoCLIP offers a scalable, clinically-focused solution for foundational vision-language modeling in ultrasound, aiming to address long-standing challenges in fetal medical imaging.

title: ConCise: Training-Free Conclusion-Chain State Compression for Cost-Efficient Multi-Step RAG Services
comment: to be published in IEEE ICWS 2026
summary: Multi-step retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has been widely deployed as LLM-powered web services for complex question answering, where iterative retrieval-reasoning rounds deliver strong multi-hop accuracy. However, this paradigm causes historical documents and reasoning traces to accumulate across rounds, inflating cumulative input tokens approximately as $O(N^2)$ with progressively increasing noise density. In API-based service architectures, such growth directly amplifies per-request billing cost, network payload, and response latency. Existing compression approaches rely on pretrained modules or GPU-level KV cache access, introducing model hosting overhead incompatible with API-native, Serverless, and edge-side deployments. To address this issue, this paper proposes ConCise, a training-free state-layer protocol that restructures cross-round context transmission for multi-step RAG services. Specifically, ConCise replaces raw-text accumulation with an append-only chain of structured conclusions, compressing cumulative context growth from $O(N^2)$ to approximately $O(N)$. Furthermore, a fused generation mechanism is introduced to jointly emit reasoning and conclusions in a single API call, eliminating repeated input billing from serial dual-invocation overhead. Extensive experiments across twelve paired configurations spanning three models, two datasets, and two representative frameworks demonstrate that ConCise achieves 64.63\% average token savings while maintaining acceptable accuracy, providing a plug-and-play, deployment-friendly solution for cost-efficient multi-step RAG service optimization.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ConCise, a training-free method for optimizing multi-step retrieval-augmented generation by compressing token growth and cutting costs while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Address the inefficiency and cost inflation caused by cumulative token growth in multi-step retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, especially within API-based services.
AI.method: Introduce ConCise, a protocol replacing raw-text accumulation with a structured chain of conclusions, achieving linear token growth ($O(N)$) and a fused generation mechanism to reduce input billing.
AI.result: ConCise demonstrated 64.63% token savings while maintaining accuracy across multiple models, datasets, and frameworks.
AI.conclusion: ConCise offers a deployment-friendly, cost-efficient solution for multi-step RAG services, compatible with various deployment environments and retaining strong performance.

title: The Undecidability of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) Alignment
summary: This article establishes the foundational mathematical limits of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) safety, proving that the core barrier is not the impossibility of an aligned state, but its structural unverifiability. We formalize this boundary through two central impossibility results: the Unverifiability Theorem of Alignment and the Theorem of Finite Structural Unverifiability of AGI Alignment. We ground this boundary at Trakhtenbrot's Wall, demonstrating that contemporary engineering defenses relying on finite hardware or halting architectures fail to escape logical obstructions. This failure manifests as an inescapable triad of containment failures: open domains yield fundamental undecidability (Rice and Gödel); universal finite verification collapses into algorithmic incomputability (Trakhtenbrot); and particular bounded environments trap the supervisor within intractable bounds in the worst case. As a direct structural corollary of these results, we derive the Soundness--Completeness--Tractability Trilemma, establishing that the mutual incompatibility of these three properties is a necessary consequence of descriptive complexity rather than an empirical anomaly. Finally, we map these theoretical bounds onto practical AI engineering, demonstrating that modern containment strategies are not temporary patches, but mandatory sacrifices of logical expressivity required to secure decidable fragments of safety.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that AGI safety is structurally unverifiable due to core logical and mathematical limits related to decision processes, highlighting the inherent barriers to establishing aligned and verifiable AGI systems.
AI.motivation: The study aims to explore and mathematically formalize the fundamental barriers to ensuring the safety and alignment of AGI, addressing whether structural obstacles prevent verified safety.
AI.method: The authors derive two key impossibility theorems and analyze their implications through theoretical frameworks such as undecidability and incomputability. They also tie these mathematical barriers to engineering challenges.
AI.result: Key results include the proof of the Unverifiability Theorem of Alignment and the Soundness--Completeness--Tractability Trilemma, which collectively show that safety and alignment in AGI are fundamentally unverifiable under current theoretical frameworks.
AI.conclusion: The conclusions demonstrate that the structural limits posed by descriptive complexity make complete AGI safety infeasible, necessitating engineering compromises that trade logical expressivity for safety and security.

title: Improved Predictive Performance and Interpretability for Mesomorphic Neural Networks Using Local Fidelity Regularization
summary:
Interpretable Mesomorphic Neural Networks (IMNs) offer a promising framework that combines the predictive power of deep neural networks with the interpretability of linear models. However, the original formulation lacks safeguards to ensure that the learned interpretations are in fact reliable. In particular, the network is free to concentrate all explanatory variance into a single weight of the linear output layer, achieving strong predictive performance while producing interpretations that are largely meaningless. Paradoxically, the L1 penalty proposed to encourage sparse solutions exacerbates this problem by further incentivizing such degenerate configurations.
  To address this vulnerability, we introduce Local Fidelity Regularization (LFR), a novel penalty term that prevents degenerate weight collapse by aligning the linear output weights with local data variations. This structural constraint guarantees faithful explanations and substantially improves the reliability of model interpretations. Furthermore, empirical evaluations across the OpenML benchmark suite demonstrate that LFR does not compromise accuracy for explainability; rather, it achieved improved AUROC over the unregularized IMN. By yielding results highly competitive with state-of-the-art black-box models, LFR provides the dual benefit of reliable interpretability and superior predictive performance. Source code and usage instructions are available at https://github.com/hugohammer/LFR-IMN.git.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Local Fidelity Regularization (LFR) to improve interpretability in mesomorphic neural networks, resolving issues with unreliable explanations caused by prior methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the problem of unreliable interpretations in Interpretable Mesomorphic Neural Networks, where prior methods allowed reliability issues such as degenerate weight collapse and meaningless explanations.
AI.method: The paper introduces LFR, a penalty term that aligns output weights with local data variations to prevent weight collapse, ensuring faithful and reliable interpretations.
AI.result: LFR significantly improves interpretability by providing reliable explanations while also achieving better AUROC compared to unregularized models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LFR method successfully balances interpretability and accuracy, providing a dual benefit of superior predictive performance and faithful model interpretations.

title: One Scene, Two Depths: Probing Geometric Ambiguity in Monocular Foundation Models
comment: 49 pages, 25 figures; Accepted by European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV) 2026
summary: A faithful 3D world representation should account for layered geometry, where a single camera ray may contain multiple visible and geometrically valid surfaces. Monocular depth estimation, however, reduces this structure to one scalar depth per pixel. Transparent scenes make this ambiguity measurable: the same ray can pass through foreground glass and observe the background, turning the supervised target into a convention of annotation, data, and training rather than a scene-intrinsic truth. A learned predictor exposes this convention as its depth-layer preference. We introduce MultiDepth-3k (MD-3k), a sparse two-layer ordinal benchmark for measuring depth-layer preference and multi-layer spatial relationship accuracy (ML-SRA). On MD-3k, leading depth foundation models exhibit diverse layer preferences under standard RGB input, showing that the same layered geometry can be resolved differently across models. We further find that Laplacian Visual Prompting (LVP), a training-free spectral input transformation, can substantially change the reported layer for certain frozen models. The strongest RGB/LVP pair, DAv2-L, reaches 75.5% ML-SRA. These results suggest that depth foundation models may express complementary geometric hypotheses that standard RGB inference leaves unexpressed. We invite the community to rethink depth supervision and evaluation through an ambiguity-aware lens, where multiple valid 3D interpretations are treated as geometric structure to be measured, preserved, and expressed.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark and method are proposed to address the multi-layer geometry problem in monocular depth estimation, highlighting its limitations and showing how depth models interpret layered scenes differently.
AI.motivation: Monocular depth estimation models traditionally reduce depth to a single scalar per pixel, which fails to account for the layered geometry of real-world scenes such as transparent objects.
AI.method: Introduced the MultiDepth-3k (MD-3k) benchmark for evaluating depth-layer preference and multi-layer spatial relationship accuracy (ML-SRA). Used Laplacian Visual Prompting (LVP) as a spectral input transformation to enhance depth model evaluation.
AI.result: Current depth models show varied performance and interpretations of layered geometry. When combining RGB with LVP, the DAv2-L model achieved 75.5% in ML-SRA, demonstrating improved multi-layer depth understanding.
AI.conclusion: Depth estimation should embrace the ambiguity of layered geometries as inherent structure to preserve and analyze. The new benchmark and findings promote deeper understanding and reconsideration of depth supervision and evaluation methods.

title: Exploiting Local Flatness for Efficient Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: Detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) data is crucial for reliable machine learning deployment. Among detection strategies, post-hoc methods are particularly attractive due to their efficiency, as they operate directly on pre-trained networks without requiring retraining. Within this paradigm, one promising direction exploits loss-landscape curvature to estimate model uncertainty; however, such methods incur substantial computational cost and rely on implicit assumptions about how landscape flatness differs between in-distribution (ID) and OOD data. In this work, we provide the first systematic investigation of this curvature discrepancy and show that OOD inputs exhibit larger Hessian curvature than ID data, with the gap widening under stronger distributional shifts. Motivated by these observations, we propose Fold, a lightweight flatness-modulated OOD detector that leverages the feature Hessian and partial feature normalization to improve ID-OOD separability while avoiding costly parameter-space curvature approximations. To optimally adapt this normalization across diverse datasets, we further introduce AutoFold, a self-supervised tuning scheme that synthesizes pseudo-OOD samples via ID logit masking for automatic calibration without requiring external data. Experiments on OOD benchmarks show that Fold outperforms prior methods, improving the average AUROC by 1.63% and reducing FPR95 by 2.30%, while maintaining computational efficiency comparable to a standard forward pass. Supported by theoretical analysis and extensive ablations, Fold provides a principled and practical solution for robust real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses challenges in detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) data and proposes a new efficient method, Fold, that leverages curvature discrepancies in loss landscapes.
AI.motivation: The study is motivated by the need for reliable OOD detection in machine learning, with a specific focus on improving lightweight, post-hoc detection strategies that do not require retraining.
AI.method: The authors investigate the curvature differences between in-distribution (ID) and OOD data, demonstrating larger curvature for OOD. They propose Fold, an OOD detector that integrates feature Hessians and partial feature normalization, and introduce AutoFold, a self-supervised tuning mechanism for adapting normalization without external data.
AI.result: Fold achieves state-of-the-art performance with a 1.63% improvement in average AUROC and a 2.30% decrease in FPR95 compared to prior methods, while maintaining computational efficiency equivalent to a standard forward pass.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Fold method successfully balances efficiency and effectiveness in OOD detection, offering a theoretically sound and experimentally validated tool for deployment-ready machine learning systems.

title: Benchmarking Geospatial Foundation Models for Agriculture Applications
summary: Geospatial foundation models pretrained on satellite imagery promise broad generalization across remote sensing tasks and regions, but their geographic transferability has not been systematically tested, especially in agriculture applications. This paper presents a controlled benchmark that evaluates three models, Prithvi, SpectralGPT, and SatMAE, on multi-temporal crop segmentation and change detection across four U.S. states, Iowa, North Carolina, California, and Minnesota. By assigning each train, validation, and test split to a separate region, we measure how well each model transfers to land it has not seen. All three degrade sharply under regional distribution shift, predicting only the most common crops while missing rare ones. We further find that fitting these models to a shared input format affects each one differently, which complicates direct architectural comparison. These results expose key limitations of current geospatial foundation models for agriculture and point to region aware evaluation as a necessary standard.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates the geographic transferability of three geospatial foundation models on crop segmentation and change detection tasks across distinct U.S. regions, highlighting their sharp performance drops in unseen areas.
AI.motivation: To assess the effectiveness and limitations of geospatial pretrained models in adapting to new regions for agricultural applications, with a focus on their geographic transferability.
AI.method: Conducted a controlled benchmark evaluation of three models (Prithvi, SpectralGPT, and SatMAE) across four distinct U.S. states. Each dataset split was assigned to a unique region to assess transfer to unseen areas.
AI.result: All tested models showed sharp performance degradation in unseen regions, often predicting common crops while failing to identify rare ones. Input format standardization also affected model behavior inconsistently.
AI.conclusion: Current geospatial models for agriculture have significant limitations in adapting to new regions. There's a need for region-aware evaluation as a standard to ensure robust performance.

title: meta-pipe: An LLM-agent pipeline for end-to-end automated systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
Objective: To describe the architecture and design rationale of meta-pipe, an open-source large language model (LLM)-agent pipeline that integrates the complete systematic review and meta-analysis (SR/MA) workflow -- from literature search through statistical analysis, manuscript generation, and quality assurance -- with mandatory human oversight at critical decision points.
  Study Design and Setting: We developed a 10-stage modular pipeline integrating Claude (Anthropic; Opus 4 for reasoning, Haiku 3.5 for classification) for LLM-assisted screening and extraction, Python (~3,600 lines of code) for automation, R (meta, metafor, gemtc, netmeta) for statistical analysis, and Quarto for manuscript rendering. Five mandatory human decision points enforce oversight. We systematically compared meta-pipe's capabilities with five existing SR automation tools based on published documentation as of March 2026.
